rationale_a: Higher effort and an advisory system instruction add cost and remain skippable guidance; they cannot make the model see authorization state that the tool never reported, so the unverified-negative root cause in the tool boundary is untouched.
choice_b: Change `search_corpus` so its tool result explicitly distinguishes a genuinely-empty result from an access failure: have the wrapper verify scope/authorization for the requested corpus and, when the empty array stems from a missing scope, return a structured tool_result with is_error=true carrying an errorCategory like "access_denied" and isRetryable metadata — reserving the empty-but-successful payload only for a confirmed authorized query that legitimately matched nothing.
rationale_b: Anthropic's agent-design guidance states an agent must distinguish a genuinely-empty result from an access failure, and that tool errors should surface structured isError/errorCategory/isRetryable context rather than being silently swallowed; encoding the authorization failure into the tool result is what lets the agent stop treating an unauthorized empty array as a confirmed negative.
choice_c: Add an iteration cap so that whenever any corpus returns zero results the orchestrator automatically re-dispatches the query two more times, and only accepts "nothing found" if all three attempts return empty.
rationale_c: Retrying a deterministically unauthorized query just reproduces the same empty array three times, and an iteration cap is a safety backstop rather than a correctness mechanism — it still treats the empty payload as the signal instead of distinguishing access failure from a true empty result.
choice_d: Have `search_corpus` detect the unauthorized case and, when scope is missing, return a normal (non-error) tool_result whose results array is empty but with an added `"note": "access may have been denied"` string field, so the agent can read the note and reconsider before concluding nothing exists.
rationale_d: The objective flaw is returning the access failure as a successful (non-error) tool_result instead of setting is_error=true with a structured errorCategory; a failed tool call must be returned as an error result and never disguised as a normal empty payload, so a free-text note inside a success response still conflates the two cases the design must separate.

stem: You are architecting a document-extraction agent at a fictional firm, Marlowe Title & Escrow, that pulls structured fields (parcel ID, grantor, grantee, recording date) out of property deeds. The deeds and a custom retrieval service live behind a remote MCP server named "deed-vault" exposed over HTTPS, and your team wants Claude Opus 4.8 to call that server's search and fetch tools directly through the Messages API's MCP connector (not via a separate self-hosted MCP client). A junior engineer adds `mcp_servers=[{type:"url", url:"https://mcp.marlowe.example/deed-vault", name:"deed-vault"}]` to the request, runs it, and gets a 400 error before any extraction happens. The pipeline must process roughly 4,000 deeds per night, so you need the wiring correct on the first call rather than discovered per-batch. What is the correct way to wire this request so Claude can actually invoke the deed-vault tools?
anti_pattern: Treating the MCP connector as configured once the remote server is declared, while omitting the matching mcp_toolset tool entry (or mismatching its name) — so Claude can "see" the server connection but has no tool surface wired into the request, which the Messages API rejects as a 400. The trap rewards partial knowledge of one of the two required pieces.
choice_a: Keep the `mcp_servers` entry and add `tools=[{type:"mcp_toolset", mcp_server_name:"deed_vault"}]`, then set `tool_choice={type:"any"}` so the model is forced to use the connector on every deed.
rationale_a: The structure is right — a server plus a matching mcp_toolset — but the objective flaw is that `mcp_server_name` is spelled "deed_vault" while the server `name` is "deed-vault"; the toolset must reference the server by its EXACT name, so this name mismatch fails to wire the tools (and the forced tool_choice is irrelevant to the wiring error).
choice_b: Keep only the `mcp_servers` entry as written, but add `output_config` with `effort:"max"` so the model invests enough reasoning to discover and connect to the server on its own.
rationale_b: Effort controls how much thinking/tool-calling the model does, not whether the MCP tools are wired into the request; with no `mcp_toolset` the 400 persists regardless of effort, since the server-only declaration is rejected before any reasoning occurs.
choice_c: Remove the `mcp_servers` field entirely and instead list each deed-vault tool individually under `tools` with a normal `input_schema`, since the MCP connector is just shorthand for hand-declared custom tools.
rationale_c: This abandons the MCP connector mechanism: the connector exists precisely so you do not hand-author each remote tool's schema, and removing `mcp_servers` means there is no managed connection to the remote server at all, so Claude cannot reach deed-vault.
choice_d: Keep the `mcp_servers` entry and ALSO add a matching toolset to `tools`: `tools=[{type:"mcp_toolset", mcp_server_name:"deed-vault"}]`, where `mcp_server_name` exactly equals the server's `name`. The MCP connector requires both the server declaration and a matching mcp_toolset referencing it by name; declaring the server alone is a 400.
rationale_d: The Messages API MCP connector is two-part: `mcp_servers` declares the connection and a sibling `tools` entry of type `mcp_toolset` whose `mcp_server_name` matches the server `name` actually exposes those tools to the model — declaring the server without the matching toolset is documented to return a 400.

stem: At Northwind Returns, a customer-support agent built on Claude triages refund tickets. Its current design routes everything through one general-purpose bash tool: the agent greps the order DB, computes the refund amount, and then runs `python send_refund_email.py --to <customer> --amount <n>` to dispatch an irreversible refund-confirmation email through the billing provider. Over 4,200 tickets last month, three incidents occurred where the agent emailed a refund confirmation for the wrong amount, and post-mortems showed the harness had no way to require human confirmation before the send because the dispatch was buried inside an opaque bash invocation. The architecture team wants the send step to become something the harness can reliably gate (require approval), mark as not parallel-safe, and surface structured errors for — while keeping cheap, high-volume read operations (DB lookups, amount math) fast and unceremonious. Which change best fits Anthropic's published agent-design guidance?
anti_pattern: Treating a hard-to-reverse, externally-visible action (sending a refund email to a customer) as just another opaque shell command, so the harness has no seam to gate, confirm, or render it — and conflating "give it a dedicated tool" with the separate, optional choices of permission gating, staleness checks, and structured error surfaces.
choice_a: Split the agent into many fine-grained tools — one each for `lookup_order`, `compute_refund`, `validate_customer`, `format_email`, `dispatch_email`, `log_refund`, and a dozen similar steps — so every operation including the send is individually visible and controllable by the harness.
rationale_a: Decomposing the cheap read/compute steps into a dozen-plus tools pushes the agent toward the ~18+ tools range that degrades tool selection; guidance favors ~4–5 focused tools and promoting only the action that genuinely needs gating, not exploding every step into its own tool.
choice_b: Promote the refund send to its own dedicated tool (e.g. `send_refund_email`) with an explicit input schema, leaving the read/compute steps in bash; because it is a discrete, hard-to-reverse, externally-visible action, the dedicated tool gives the harness a seam to gate it behind approval, mark it not parallel-safe, and return structured errors — none of which is possible while it is hidden inside an opaque bash call.
rationale_b: Public guidance says to promote an action from bash to a dedicated tool precisely when you must gate hard-to-reverse actions, mark it parallel-safe (or not), or render/handle it specially; bash gives breadth but is opaque to the harness, so it cannot gate or specially handle a step buried inside it.
choice_c: Keep the send inside the general-purpose bash tool, but add a CLAUDE.md instruction telling the agent to always ask the user for confirmation before running the refund-email command, since CLAUDE.md persists project conventions across every turn.
rationale_c: A CLAUDE.md instruction is advisory and can be skipped by the model, so it cannot reliably gate a hard-to-reverse action; deterministic enforcement requires a harness seam (a dedicated tool the harness can gate, or a hook), not a prompt convention.
choice_d: Promote the refund send to a dedicated `send_refund_email` tool and have its handler, on any billing-provider failure, return a tool_result whose text is "Operation failed" so the agent can retry, keeping the read/compute steps in bash.
rationale_d: Promoting the irreversible send to a dedicated gated tool is correct, but the failure handler is wrong: public guidance says tool errors should be a structured payload (isError / errorCategory / isRetryable plus context), not a generic "Operation failed" string, which is the generic-error anti-pattern the scenario explicitly wants to avoid.

stem: At Meridian Filings, an extraction agent ingests roughly 4,200 regulatory PDFs per night, parses each into a structured JSON record, and writes the record back to a shared `records/` tree that human analysts also edit concurrently during the day. The team is on Claude Opus 4.8 and currently performs all writes via a single broad `bash` tool, instructing the model in the system prompt: "Before overwriting any record, re-read the file and confirm it has not changed since you last read it; if it changed, skip the write." Last week three analyst edits were silently clobbered when the agent overwrote files that had been modified between its read and its write, and a post-mortem found the model had occasionally skipped the re-read step under load. The architect wants writes to be impossible whenever the on-disk file has changed since the agent last read it, and wants the rejection to be visible to the orchestration harness so it can branch on it. Which design best meets these requirements?
anti_pattern: Trying to enforce a hard, hard-to-reverse safety condition (don't overwrite a file modified since you last read it) through advisory prompt text or opaque shell commands, rather than encoding it as a structural property of a dedicated, harness-visible tool that can reject the call deterministically before any write occurs.
choice_a: Keep writing through bash, but after each nightly run start a second same-session review pass where the same agent re-reads every record it wrote and rolls back any that overwrote a concurrent analyst edit.
rationale_a: This is purely reactive — the clobbering write already happened and may be unrecoverable — and same-session self-review carries the model's own reasoning bias; it neither prevents the stale write nor gives the harness a pre-write gate to branch on.
choice_b: Replace the broad bash write with a dedicated `edit_record` tool whose implementation re-reads the file and compares it against the content/hash the agent last read, and returns a structured tool_result with is_error=true (e.g. errorCategory "stale_write") when they differ — performing the write only when they match. The staleness check is enforced in the tool code, so it runs on every call regardless of the model's reasoning, and the structured error lets the harness branch deterministically.
rationale_b: Promote an action to a dedicated tool precisely when you must gate a hard-to-reverse action or enforce a staleness check; the condition then lives in deterministic tool code rather than advisory prompt text, and a structured is_error/errorCategory result gives the harness a checkable signal to branch on.
choice_c: Replace the broad bash write with a dedicated `edit_record` tool that re-reads the file and compares it against the last-read content, but when they differ have the tool skip the write and return a normal (non-error) tool_result whose text says "write skipped: file changed since last read."
rationale_c: The dedicated tool with an in-code staleness check is correct, but returning the skip as a normal (non-error) tool_result instead of one with is_error=true is the objective flaw: it conflates a genuine refusal with a successful write, so the harness cannot reliably distinguish a stale-rejection from a completed edit and may treat the failure as success.
choice_d: Keep the single broad bash write tool but strengthen the system prompt with an emphatic, numbered instruction ("STEP 1: ALWAYS re-read and diff the file; STEP 2: NEVER write if it changed"), and add a few-shot example of correctly skipping a changed file, so the model reliably performs the check itself.
rationale_d: A prompt instruction is advisory and can be skipped by the model — which is exactly how the clobbers happened under load; only deterministic/programmatic enforcement (tool code or a hook) can make the unsafe write actually impossible.

stem: You are architecting a multi-step Claude agent for Meridian Title that extracts 14 fields (parcel ID, grantor, lien amounts, etc.) from county-recorder PDFs via a custom `fetch_recorded_doc` tool backed by a flaky third-party API. The tool can fail three distinct ways: the recorder API returns HTTP 503 (transient), the document ID is malformed (the model should fix its argument and not retry blindly), and the document genuinely has no lien section (a real, valid empty). Today the tool catches every exception and returns the same `"Operation failed"` string, so Claude retries 503s and malformed-ID errors identically, loops on un-fixable inputs, and sometimes reports "no liens found" when the API was actually down. You are redesigning the tool's failure contract so the agent loop can branch correctly. Which design best follows Anthropic's guidance for tool error reporting?
anti_pattern: A tool that fails returns a single opaque "Operation failed" string (or worse, an empty success-shaped result), giving the model no machine-readable signal to decide whether to retry, escalate, or treat the result as a real empty. Structured errors carry isError plus an errorCategory and isRetryable so the agent loop can branch deterministically instead of guessing from prose.
choice_a: Return a structured error object on failure carrying `isError: true`, an `errorCategory` (e.g. `transient_upstream`, `invalid_argument`, `not_found`) and an `isRetryable` boolean, plus a human-readable message; and on a genuinely empty lien section return a normal (non-error) success result that explicitly represents "zero liens" so the model can distinguish a real empty from an access failure.
rationale_a: Anthropic's agent-design guidance is that tool errors should be structured with isError/errorCategory/isRetryable plus context (never a generic "Operation failed"), and that a genuinely-empty result must be distinguishable from an access failure — exactly the signals the loop needs to retry transient errors, fix arguments on invalid_argument, and trust real empties.
choice_b: Return a structured error with `errorCategory` and `isRetryable` and a descriptive message for the three failure modes, and for the genuinely-empty lien section also return that same structured error shape with `isError: true`, `errorCategory: "not_found"`, and `isRetryable: false` so empties flow through one consistent error path.
rationale_b: The structured-error design for the three failures is correct, but encoding a genuinely-empty (valid) lien section as `isError: true` collapses "real empty" into "failure" — the exact empty-as-failure confusion Anthropic warns against; a true empty must be a normal success result, not an error.
choice_c: Keep returning the single `"Operation failed"` string for all failures, but prepend a category prefix into the message text (e.g. `"Operation failed: 503"` vs `"Operation failed: bad id"`) so Claude can read the category from the prose and decide whether to retry, avoiding a schema change.
rationale_c: This is the generic-error anti-pattern lightly disguised: forcing the model to parse retry/category intent out of natural-language prose is fragile and is exactly what structured isError/errorCategory/isRetryable fields exist to replace.
choice_d: Drop in-tool error reporting entirely; instead lower the failure rate by raising the agent's `effort` to `xhigh` and adding a fixed cap of 3 retries in the orchestration loop, so transient 503s are absorbed by retries and malformed IDs simply exhaust the cap.
rationale_d: Raising effort does not give the model failure signals, and a blind fixed retry cap re-runs un-fixable malformed-ID calls until exhaustion (wasting turns) while still never letting the model distinguish a real empty from an outage — it sidesteps the actual contract problem.

stem: You are designing a multi-agent research assistant for a fictional firm, Brightmoor Analytics. A lead Claude Opus 4.8 agent orchestrates two retrieval tools that its subagents share: `search_internal_memos` (queries ~48,000 indexed company memos) and `search_public_filings` (queries an external EDGAR-style corpus of regulatory filings). In testing, the agent frequently calls `search_public_filings` for questions that should hit internal memos and vice-versa, and sometimes calls both redundantly before answering. Both tool descriptions currently read like "Searches the [internal memo / public filings] corpus and returns the top matching documents." The orchestration prompt already explains, at length, the difference between the two corpora and when each should be used. An architect proposes a fix focused on the tool definitions themselves. Which change is the most effective and correctly-implemented way to reduce the wrong-tool and redundant-call behavior?
anti_pattern: Writing a tool description that only restates the tool's mechanical action ("what it does") and relying on the system prompt or the model's judgment to decide when to invoke it, instead of encoding the selection criteria — when to use it and when NOT to — into the tool description itself, where the model actually reads them at selection time. A related trap is treating tool overlap as something to fix by adding more enforcement (tool_choice, more tools) rather than by disambiguating the descriptions.
choice_a: Keep the descriptions as purely "what it does" text, but set `tool_choice` to `{type: "any"}` on the orchestration call so the model is forced to pick exactly one of the two retrieval tools, which prevents it from calling both redundantly.
rationale_a: `tool_choice:{type:"any"}` only forces the model to use SOME tool — it does not restrict it to a single call, and it does nothing to fix WHICH tool is chosen; the redundant-call symptom is suppressed by `disable_parallel_tool_use:true`, not by `any`, and the wrong-tool problem is untouched because the disambiguating criteria still aren't in the descriptions.
choice_b: Rewrite each tool's `description` field to state when to call it and when not to — e.g., `search_internal_memos`: "Use for questions about Brightmoor's own decisions, strategy, or internal discussion. Do NOT use for external regulatory data — use search_public_filings for that." — and apply the symmetric guidance to `search_public_filings`, so the selection criteria live where the model reads them at tool-selection time.
rationale_b: A tool's description is the primary signal the model uses to decide whether to call it, so usage criteria ("when to use / when not to use," including cross-references to the sibling tool) belong in the description itself, not only in the orchestration prompt; this is exactly how you disambiguate overlapping tools.
choice_c: Leave both tool descriptions unchanged and instead expand the orchestration system prompt with even more detailed rules about which corpus maps to which question type, since the system prompt is the authoritative place to encode routing logic.
rationale_c: The orchestration prompt already contains this guidance and the wrong-tool behavior persists, because selection logic placed only in the system prompt competes with everything else there; the criteria need to be in the tool descriptions the model consults when choosing a tool.
choice_d: Merge the two tools into a single `search_documents` tool with a `corpus` enum parameter, and add a third tool, a `corpus_router`, that the agent must call first to decide which corpus to query.
rationale_d: This adds tools and an extra mandatory hop rather than clarifying selection criteria; a router tool just relocates the same ambiguous "which corpus" decision, and growing the tool count works against keeping a small, well-described tool set.

stem: You are architecting a document-extraction pipeline at a fictional firm, Brightleaf Records, that processes roughly 40,000 scanned invoices per night. A single tool named `record_invoice` receives the extracted fields (vendor, invoice_number, total_cents, due_date) and writes them to a downstream ledger. Last week the on-call engineer found 312 invoices were rejected by the ledger because Claude occasionally emitted an extra `notes` field and sometimes omitted `due_date`, producing tool inputs that drifted from the agreed schema. You want the API to guarantee every `record_invoice` call conforms exactly to the schema — no extra keys, all four fields present — without adding a post-hoc validation layer. Using Claude Opus 4.8 via the Messages API, where and how should you configure strict schema enforcement?
anti_pattern: Treating strict schema validation as a request-level routing control (placing it on tool_choice) rather than a property of the tool's own schema, and omitting the JSON Schema constraints (additionalProperties:false + required) that strict mode requires to actually validate inputs.
choice_a: Leave the tool definition unchanged and instead set `output_config.format` to a JSON schema matching the four fields, which forces all model output to conform to the contract.
rationale_a: output_config.format constrains the assistant's structured text output, not the inputs to a tool call, so it does not guarantee that `record_invoice` tool inputs conform to the schema.
choice_b: Set `strict: true` as a sibling of `name`/`description`/`input_schema` on the `record_invoice` tool definition, and make the tool's `input_schema` enforce the contract by adding `additionalProperties: false` and listing all four fields in `required`.
rationale_b: strict mode is a property of the tool definition itself (sibling of name/description/input_schema) and only validates as intended when the schema also declares additionalProperties:false and required, which together forbid the extra `notes` key and force `due_date` to be present.
choice_c: Set `tool_choice: {type: "tool", name: "record_invoice", strict: true}` so the forced tool selection also enforces the input schema on every call.
rationale_c: strict is not a field on tool_choice; tool_choice only controls which/whether a tool is selected, so placing strict there does not enable schema validation (and the wrong placement embodies the core trap of this topic).
choice_d: Add `strict: true` to the `record_invoice` tool definition alongside `name` and `input_schema`, but leave the schema as-is with only the four field types declared, since marking the tool strict is what activates exact-conformance validation.
rationale_d: The placement is correct (strict belongs on the tool definition), but the objective flaw is that strict mode relies on the schema's own constraints — without additionalProperties:false the extra `notes` key is still allowed, and without listing the fields in required the missing `due_date` is not caught.

stem: Meridian Labs runs a managed-agent research pipeline: a single "LitScout" Agent (created once, model claude-opus-4-8) is referenced by roughly 240 Sessions/day, each fanning out across three remote MCP servers — an internal patents index, a licensed journals API, and a clinical-trials registry — all reached via the Messages API MCP connector. The journals provider rotates its OAuth bearer token every 14 days, and Meridian's security team requires that a credential rotation never force a redeploy of agent logic and never expose secrets in the agent's stored configuration. A new architect proposes encoding all three MCP servers' tokens directly in the Agent's mcp_servers configuration at agents.create time so every Session inherits them automatically. The team asks you to choose the design that satisfies the rotation and secret-handling requirements while keeping the agent definition stable. Which approach is correct?
anti_pattern: Treating the managed Agent object as the place to hold MCP server authentication. Because the agent definition (model/system/tools/mcp_servers/skills) is created once and referenced from every session, baking a credential into it couples a rotating secret to an immutable, shared, permanent-on-archive object — instead of attaching auth through a vault that can be swapped without touching the agent.
choice_a: Keep the Agent definition free of credentials; store the three MCP servers' auth in one or more Vaults and attach them to each Session via vault_ids. When the journals token rotates every 14 days, update the vault's secret — the shared Agent definition is never touched and no redeploy of agent logic occurs.
rationale_a: For managed agents, MCP auth lives in vaults attached via vault_ids and never in the agent definition; the agent is created once and referenced by ID, so rotating the secret inside the vault leaves the immutable, shared agent definition untouched and requires no redeploy.
choice_b: Adopt the architect's proposal: encode all three MCP tokens directly in the Agent's mcp_servers configuration at agents.create time so every Session inherits them, and re-run agents.create with the new token whenever the journals provider rotates.
rationale_b: Embedding the tokens in the Agent's mcp_servers configuration puts secrets in the stored agent definition and ties rotation to recreating the create-once shared agent, violating both the no-secrets-in-config and stable-definition requirements; MCP auth is meant to live in vaults.
choice_c: Keep credentials out of the Agent definition and store the MCP auth in a Vault, but attach the vault by calling agents.create again for each rotation so the new Agent picks up the refreshed vault binding, then point new Sessions at the new Agent ID.
rationale_c: Storing auth in a vault is correct, but the objective flaw is calling agents.create for every rotation — an Agent must be created once and referenced by ID, and the vault's secret can be updated in place, so recreating the agent (and churning its ID) is both unnecessary and contrary to the create-once guidance.
choice_d: Leave the Agent and vaults out of it: have each Session pass the three bearer tokens inline in the mcp_servers array on every Messages API request, so rotation just means sending the new token on the next call.
rationale_d: Passing raw bearer tokens inline in the mcp_servers array on every request scatters the secret across all 240 daily call sites and bypasses the vault mechanism designed to hold MCP auth, making centralized rotation and secret handling harder, not easier.

stem: You are architecting a multi-agent research system on Claude Opus 4.8. A lead orchestrator agent dispatches a `fetch_corpus` MCP tool that pulls full-text filings from an internal document service; for a typical query it returns roughly 140K tokens of raw filings across ~60 documents, but the agent usually only needs two or three specific sections to answer. With the raw result streamed straight back into the orchestrator's message history, every subsequent turn re-sends the entire payload, cache_read input balloons, and the orchestrator's reasoning quality visibly degrades as the window fills. The team needs the orchestrator to still be able to consult any part of the corpus when a follow-up demands it, without the full text ever sitting in its context. Which design best fits Anthropic's published guidance for handling large tool results?
anti_pattern: Routing an oversized tool payload back through the model's context window (or trying to "fix" it with a lossy summarization pass) instead of keeping the bulk out of context entirely and letting the agent read only the slice it needs on demand. Treating /compact-style summarization, a bigger context model, or returning everything-and-letting-the-model-cope as substitutes for never putting the 100K+ payload in context in the first place.
choice_a: Have `fetch_corpus` return all 60 documents but split the 140K tokens across multiple sequential tool_result messages so no single message exceeds a size limit, letting the orchestrator process them one message at a time.
rationale_a: Chunking the payload across messages still places the entire 140K tokens into the orchestrator's context history and re-sends it on subsequent turns; it solves a non-existent per-message limit while leaving the actual context-bloat and cache-cost problem fully intact.
choice_b: Leave `fetch_corpus` unchanged and switch the orchestrator to Claude Opus 4.8's 1M-token context window so all ~140K tokens fit comfortably with room to spare, eliminating the degradation without changing the tool.
rationale_b: A larger context window does not address the core problem: the full payload is re-sent every turn (inflating cache_read cost) and a filling window still degrades reasoning, so "it fits" is not the same as "it belongs in context." Anthropic's guidance is to keep large intermediate results out of context, not to grow the window to absorb them.
choice_c: Keep `fetch_corpus` returning the full 140K tokens inline, but add a hook that fires after the tool result and runs `/compact` to summarize the message history whenever the window grows large, so the orchestrator can still reference the corpus from the compacted summary on later turns.
rationale_c: This still routes the entire 140K-token payload through context first, and /compact is explicitly reactive and lossy — not a substitute for proactively isolating verbose work — so the offloaded sections it claims to preserve are exactly what compaction discards, defeating the "consult any part later" requirement.
choice_d: Have `fetch_corpus` write the full 140K-token result to a file in the agent's workspace and return only a short reference (path plus a manifest of section headers/offsets); the orchestrator then reads just the specific slices it needs on demand — ideally via programmatic tool calling, where Claude writes a script that calls the tools so the large intermediate data stays out of the model's context entirely.
rationale_d: Anthropic's agent-design guidance is to keep large intermediate results out of context: offload the payload to a file and read only the needed slice on demand, and use programmatic tool calling so the bulk of the data never enters the model's context window. This preserves both the prompt cache prefix and reasoning quality while still allowing any section to be retrieved later.

stem: Helios Research runs a multi-agent literature-review system on Claude Opus 4.8. The orchestrator agent has access to a registry of 240 specialized retrieval and citation tools, but any single research turn typically needs only 4–6 of them. The team's frozen system prompt (a 9,000-token methodology brief) and a stable, alphabetically-sorted tool list are placed at the front of every request with a cache_control breakpoint, and they have verified healthy cache_read_input_tokens across turns. An engineer proposes reducing input cost further by having the orchestrator compute, on each turn, exactly which 4–6 tool schemas that turn needs and sending ONLY those in the tools array — arguing that a smaller tools block means fewer input tokens. The lead architect objects that this will hurt, not help. Which approach correctly reduces the per-turn schema cost while preserving the cache, and why?
anti_pattern: Mutating the live tool set per request to "load only what's needed" — swapping or rewriting the tools block on every call — which sits at the front of the cache prefix and so invalidates the entire tools→system→messages cache, the opposite of the intended savings. Tool search is the documented mechanism that appends schemas on demand while leaving the cached tool definitions untouched.
choice_a: Have the orchestrator send only the 4–6 needed tool schemas per turn, but keep them alphabetically sorted and move the cache_control breakpoint to sit after the system prompt so the system brief still benefits from caching even as the tools change.
rationale_a: The objective flaw: tools render BEFORE system in the cache prefix, so changing the tools array per turn invalidates everything after it — including the system prompt — regardless of where the breakpoint is placed; you cannot cache content that sits behind a mutated prefix. Sorting the subset does not help because the SET of tools still differs each turn.
choice_b: Keep the full, stably-sorted tool definitions in the cached prefix and enable tool search, which appends only the relevant tool schemas on demand at request time; because the cached tools block is never mutated, the tools→system→messages prefix stays cache-eligible and cache_read_input_tokens remains high.
rationale_b: Tool search loads only relevant schemas on demand while leaving the cached tool definitions in place, so the front of the prefix is unchanged and the whole tools→system→messages cache survives; this is the documented way to scale a large tool registry without re-caching it every turn.
choice_c: Switch the orchestrator to Claude Haiku 4.5 for tool-selection turns to cut cost, sending the full 240-tool array each time since the cheaper model absorbs the larger input token price.
rationale_c: Changing the model invalidates the entire cache (the model is part of what the cache is keyed on), so switching mid-session forces a full re-cache and still pays to re-send all 240 schemas; this addresses neither the schema cost nor the caching goal.
choice_d: Move the volatile per-turn tool subset to the very front of the request and place the frozen system brief last, so the smallest, most-changing content is isolated at the start of the prefix.
rationale_d: This inverts the caching rule: stable content (frozen system, sorted tools) must come first and volatile content last, because any byte change in the prefix invalidates everything after it — putting volatile tools first guarantees the cache is busted every turn.

stem: You are architecting a document-extraction pipeline at a fictional firm, Larchmont Title, that parses ~4,200 scanned property deeds per night and must emit each result as a strictly-shaped JSON object (fixed keys: grantor, grantee, parcel_id, recording_date). The original pipeline ran on Claude 3.5-era models and guaranteed well-formed JSON by prefilling the assistant turn with an opening brace (`{`) so the model could only continue a JSON object. After migrating the calls to Claude Opus 4.8 (claude-opus-4-8), the team finds that every request now returns an HTTP 400 error before any tokens are generated, and the nightly batch fails wholesale. The lead engineer wants the most direct, supported fix that preserves the hard guarantee of schema-conformant JSON without relying on post-hoc string repair. Which change should the architect make?
anti_pattern: The trap is reaching for assistant-message prefill (`"role":"assistant"` with an opening `{`) to coerce JSON — a once-idiomatic technique that is now removed on current Opus/Fable models and returns a 400. The near-miss embodies a subtler version: knowing prefill is gone but reaching for the deprecated top-level `output_format` field or putting `strict` in the wrong place, instead of the current `output_config.format` with a `json_schema`.
choice_a: Set `strict: true` inside `tool_choice` and define a single extraction tool, forcing the model to return arguments matching the schema as the guaranteed-JSON path.
rationale_a: `strict` belongs on the tool definition (a sibling of name/description/input_schema), not inside `tool_choice`, so this is an invalid configuration; for direct JSON output `output_config.format` is the intended mechanism rather than a forced tool call.
choice_b: Remove the now-unsupported assistant prefill and instead pass a top-level `output_format` field with the JSON schema for the four fields, which restores guaranteed structured output on the migrated model.
rationale_b: The objective flaw is the field name and location: the top-level `output_format` parameter is deprecated and has been superseded by `output_config.format`. Correctly removing the prefill and correctly choosing schema-based structured output, but naming the deprecated top-level field, makes this option technically wrong.
choice_c: Keep the assistant-turn prefill but move it into a `system` instruction that says "begin your reply with {", since Opus 4.8 only rejects prefill when it appears in the messages array, and add a regex post-processor to repair any malformed JSON.
rationale_c: This misstates the mechanism: prefill is removed on Opus 4.8 regardless of how it is phrased, and relying on regex repair abandons the hard guarantee; a system hint plus string repair is exactly the fragile post-hoc approach the architect wants to avoid.
choice_d: Remove the assistant-turn prefill entirely (it is no longer supported on Opus 4.8 and is what triggers the 400), and instead set `output_config.format` to a `json_schema` describing the four fields, so the platform enforces the structured shape natively.
rationale_d: Assistant-turn prefill was removed on Opus 4.6/4.7/4.8 and Fable 5 and now returns a 400, so it must be deleted; the current supported mechanism for guaranteed JSON is `output_config.format` with a `json_schema`, which enforces the shape at the platform level rather than via fragile string continuation.

stem: You are architecting a multi-agent research assistant on Claude Opus 4.8. Each research subagent answers a sub-question by reading from three independent read-only sources: a 40,000-document vector index, a corporate wiki, and a SQL warehouse of 1,200 tables. In the current design all three reads go through a single general-purpose `run_shell` tool that shells out to different scripts, and runs are noticeably slow because the subagent issues the three reads strictly one after another even though none depends on the others. The lead architect wants the harness to dispatch the three independent reads concurrently within a single assistant turn, while keeping a hard-to-reverse `write_report` action gated and sequential. Which change best lets the harness schedule the three reads in parallel?
anti_pattern: The traps confuse WHERE parallelism is enabled: hiding read-only actions inside one opaque bash tool (the harness can't schedule what it can't individually see), or steering scheduling through advisory prompt text / thinking-effort knobs instead of the tool definitions and tool_choice settings that actually govern concurrent dispatch. One trap also flips on disable_parallel_tool_use, which caps the assistant at one tool call per turn and silently defeats the promotion.
choice_a: Leave the tools unchanged and instead raise the subagent's effort to `xhigh` and enable adaptive thinking, so the model reasons hard enough to overlap the three reads on its own.
rationale_a: Effort and adaptive thinking control how much the model thinks and how many tool calls it is willing to make, not whether the harness treats individual actions as parallel-safe; concurrency comes from dedicated parallel-safe tool definitions plus parallel-enabled tool_choice, not from thinking budget.
choice_b: Promote each read into its own dedicated tool (`search_vector_index`, `read_wiki`, `query_warehouse`), each defined as a discrete read-only/parallel-safe action, and leave `tool_choice` at its default (`auto`, parallel tool use enabled); the harness can then emit all three tool_use blocks in one assistant message and you return all three tool_results in a single following user message.
rationale_b: A single bash/`run_shell` tool is opaque to the harness, which can only schedule actions it can see individually; promoting each read into its own dedicated, parallel-safe tool exposes three distinct invocable actions. With `tool_choice` left at its default (`auto`, parallel tool use enabled), the model can emit all three independent tool_use blocks in one assistant message, and you return all three tool_results in one following user message — which is exactly how concurrent dispatch works. The hard-to-reverse `write_report` action stays a separate gated tool and is unaffected, so it remains sequential.
choice_c: Promote each read into its own dedicated read-only tool, then set `tool_choice` to `{type: "any", disable_parallel_tool_use: true}` so the subagent is forced to keep calling the new read tools until all three sources are covered.
rationale_c: Promoting the reads to dedicated tools is exactly right, but `disable_parallel_tool_use: true` caps the assistant at one tool call per response, so the three reads are forced back into sequential turns and the promotion's parallelism is silently defeated.
choice_d: Keep the single `run_shell` tool but add a system-prompt instruction telling the subagent to always fire the vector, wiki, and warehouse reads at the same time before doing anything else.
rationale_d: A single bash tool stays opaque to the harness so it cannot schedule its invocations as distinct parallel-safe actions, and a prompt instruction is advisory and can be skipped — it does not change how the harness dispatches tool calls.

stem: A research agent at Halcyon Labs uses the Claude Messages API with the `web_search_20260209` server tool to gather sources on niche regulatory topics. During a run against an Opus 4.8 model, one query exhausts the configured `max_uses` budget. The engineer wrapped the `client.messages.create(...)` call in a try/except (try/catch) block, expecting a failed web search to raise an exception they could log and retry. Instead, no exception fires — the request returns normally — yet downstream code that reads the search results throws when it tries to iterate them. How should the engineer handle a web-search tool failure correctly?
anti_pattern: Treating an Anthropic server-side tool (web search / web fetch) failure as a thrown/HTTP exception, so the handler wraps the API call in try/except and never inspects the returned result block — meaning genuine tool errors (which arrive as a normal 200 with an error object inside the result content) slip through as if the call succeeded, or the code crashes trying to index an error object as a results list.
choice_a: Keep the try/except but catch `anthropic.APIStatusError` specifically; a web-search budget overrun returns an HTTP 429, so catching the status error lets you read `retry-after` and back off.
rationale_a: A `max_uses_exceeded` server-tool failure is not an HTTP-level error and does not produce a 429 or any `APIStatusError` — the request succeeds with status 200, so no exception is ever raised to catch, and `retry-after` is irrelevant.
choice_b: Set `tool_choice` to `{"type": "tool", "name": "web_search", "strict": true}` so the API validates the tool call and raises a 400 when the search cannot complete, turning the failure into a catchable exception.
rationale_b: `strict` belongs on a tool definition (for input-schema validation), not on `tool_choice`, and neither mechanism converts a server-tool runtime failure into a raised exception — server-tool errors are always returned as result-block data, never as a 400.
choice_c: The web search failure surfaces inside the response as a `web_search_tool_result` block, so inspect the result block — but detect the failure by checking whether the block's `content` list is empty, treating a zero-length results list as the error signal.
rationale_c: This correctly abandons the try/except and inspects the result block, but the failure detection is objectively wrong: on error the block's `content` is an error object (e.g. `{"error_code": ...}`), not an empty list, and an empty list is a legitimate "no results found" success — so this both misclassifies real errors and conflates them with empty-but-successful searches.
choice_d: The web search returns a successful HTTP 200 response containing a `web_search_tool_result` block whose `content` is a single error object (e.g. `{"error_code": "max_uses_exceeded"}`) rather than a list of results. Inspect each `web_search_tool_result` block and branch on whether its `content` is an error object versus a results list — the error never surfaces as a raised exception.
rationale_d: Anthropic server-side tools run inside the model turn and report failures as data: a normal 200 response with an error object inside the tool-result block. The try/except never fires because nothing is raised, so error handling must inspect the result block's `content` shape (object = error, list = success).

stem: You are the architect for "LedgerLift," a batch invoice-extraction agent that classifies each of ~40,000 scanned invoices into exactly one of nine accounting buckets (e.g., "cogs", "opex", "payroll", "tax_withholding"). The model is Claude Sonnet 4.6, and you expose a single tool, record_classification, whose input_schema declares a "bucket" property as a string with an "enum" listing the nine allowed bucket ids, plus a free-text "evidence" field. During a 2,000-invoice pilot, a downstream ledger import job occasionally rejects a row because the bucket value does not match any of the nine ids (e.g., the model emitted "cost_of_goods" instead of "cogs"). Your finance lead insists the enum should make out-of-set values "structurally impossible," and proposes simply re-emphasizing the allowed ids in the tool description. As the architect, what is the most correct way to guarantee that every recorded bucket is one of the nine ids, and how should the agent loop handle a value that still falls outside the set?
anti_pattern: Believing that an enum declared in a tool's input_schema is, by itself, hard-enforced at the API boundary — and conflating schema declaration with schema validation. The trap is assuming that listing allowed values guarantees the model can never emit anything else, and that no downstream validation or strict-mode enforcement is required.
choice_a: Set strict:true on the record_classification tool definition (as a sibling of name/description/input_schema) so the API constrains generation to the schema, and ensure the input_schema has additionalProperties:false with "bucket" marked required; additionally, parse each tool input with json.loads and, if a bucket value is ever still out of the nine-id set, return that tool_result with is_error:true so the model can self-correct rather than letting the bad value reach the ledger import.
rationale_a: A bare enum in the input_schema documents the allowed set but is not guaranteed hard-enforced; strict:true on the tool definition is what makes the API constrain generation to the schema, and it requires additionalProperties:false plus required fields — with an is_error:true tool_result as the validation backstop for any value that still escapes.
choice_b: Drop the enum from the tool schema entirely and instead detect out-of-set buckets by raw-string-matching the serialized tool-call input against the nine id strings before forwarding; if no match is found, silently substitute the closest id and continue the loop without surfacing an error.
rationale_b: Tool inputs must be parsed with json.loads/JSON.parse rather than raw-string-matched, and silently substituting a guessed value masks failures instead of letting the model self-correct via an is_error:true tool_result.
choice_c: Keep the enum as the sole enforcement mechanism and re-emphasize the nine allowed ids in the tool's description text; since the enum is declared in the input_schema, any value the model emits is structurally guaranteed to be one of the nine ids, so no downstream validation or error handling is needed.
rationale_c: A declared enum documents intent but is not by itself a hard guarantee at the API boundary, and prose emphasis is advisory; relying on it alone is exactly the anti-pattern that let "cost_of_goods" reach the ledger import.
choice_d: Add strict:true inside the tool_choice object ({type:"tool", name:"record_classification", strict:true}) so generation is constrained to the enum, keep additionalProperties:false and "bucket" required in the input_schema, and on any out-of-set value return a tool_result with is_error:true so the model retries.
rationale_d: The validation backstop and schema requirements are right, but the objective flaw is placement: strict:true belongs on the TOOL definition (sibling of name/description/input_schema), not inside the tool_choice object — putting it on tool_choice does not turn on strict schema enforcement.

stem: You are architecting the orchestrator loop for "Atlas," a multi-agent research assistant built on Claude Sonnet 4.6 via the Messages API. For a single research step, Atlas issues four read-only retrieval tools in one assistant message — `search_arxiv`, `search_pubmed`, `search_internal_kb`, and `fetch_url` — emitting four `tool_use` blocks in that one response. Your retrieval layer runs the four calls concurrently against different backends, so they complete in nondeterministic order (PubMed routinely finishes last, and `fetch_url` occasionally throws a timeout). A junior engineer's first implementation streams each result back to the model the instant its backend returns: it appends one `tool_result` to a fresh `user` message per completion, correlates each result to its call by matching the Nth `tool_result` to the Nth `tool_use` in submission order, and on the `fetch_url` timeout it simply omits that block so the conversation "stays clean." In code review you notice Atlas frequently reasons over the wrong retrieval payloads and sometimes stalls. Which change correctly fixes the tool-result protocol for this parallel step?
anti_pattern: Treating parallel tool calls as independent serial turns: splitting their tool_results across multiple user messages and/or pairing results to calls by array position instead of by tool_use_id. This breaks correlation when results arrive out of order or are batched, and degrades the model's parallel-tool-use behavior.
choice_a: Keep streaming each result back the instant its backend returns — one `tool_result` per `user` message — but fix correlation by stamping each `tool_result` with the matching `tool_use_id`, and drop the `fetch_url` block on timeout since a missing result is equivalent to an empty search.
rationale_a: Splitting the results of parallel `tool_use` blocks across multiple `user` messages degrades parallel tool use, and silently omitting the timed-out tool's `tool_result` leaves a `tool_use` unanswered (stalling the loop) while conflating an error with a genuinely empty result. Correct `tool_use_id` stamping alone does not repair these two violations.
choice_b: Wait for all four backends, then return all four `tool_result` blocks together in one `user` message — but keep correlating each `tool_result` to its call by submission position (Nth result to Nth `tool_use`), and for the `fetch_url` timeout still emit an error `tool_result` with `is_error: true`.
rationale_b: The objective flaw is correlating results to calls by array position instead of by `tool_use_id`. Even when all results are batched into one message, the API binds each `tool_result` to its `tool_use` solely via the matching `tool_use_id`; position-based pairing mis-routes payloads whenever the assembled order differs from submission order, which is exactly what produces "reasoning over the wrong payload."
choice_c: Collect all four outcomes, then return them as four `tool_result` blocks inside a SINGLE `user` message, each carrying the `tool_use_id` of the `tool_use` block it answers; for the `fetch_url` timeout, include a `tool_result` with that call's `tool_use_id` and `is_error: true` rather than omitting it.
rationale_c: Multiple parallel `tool_use` blocks in one assistant message must be answered by ALL their `tool_result` blocks in ONE following `user` message, and each result is bound to its call by `tool_use_id` (never by position); a failed tool is reported with `is_error: true`, never silently dropped. This makes correlation order-independent and keeps the loop from stalling on a missing block.
choice_d: Set `disable_parallel_tool_use: true` so the four retrievals are forced into separate turns, then answer each one with a single `tool_result` (matched by `tool_use_id`) in its own `user` message; on the `fetch_url` timeout return `is_error: true`.
rationale_d: Disabling parallel tool use sidesteps rather than fixes the protocol and discards the concurrency the architecture was built for, serializing four independent read-only retrievals into four round-trips. The real defect is how parallel results are batched and correlated, not that the model emitted parallel calls.

stem: Northwind Intelligence runs a multi-agent competitive-research system on the Claude API. A long-lived "analyst" agent uses the client-side memory tool (`{"type": "memory_20250818", "name": "memory"}`) to carry findings across sessions; the team implements the storage backend themselves, persisting each `/memories` file to a shared S3 bucket that several analysts and an audit pipeline can later read. During research the agent routinely ingests scraped pages and internal CRM exports that contain customer names, email addresses, and the occasional leaked API key — and it has begun writing some of that raw material verbatim into memory files via `create` and `str_replace`. Security needs a guarantee that secrets and PII never land in the persisted store, even if a scraped page contains text engineered to make the agent save it. Which design gives that guarantee?
anti_pattern: Treating a system-prompt instruction ("never write secrets to memory") as if it were an enforcement control. A prompt instruction is advisory — the model can ignore or be steered past it — so it cannot guarantee that secrets and PII stay out of the persisted memory store. The durable control must live in the client-side memory backend (the tool handler the architect implements), which deterministically inspects and redacts every write before it touches disk.
choice_a: In the memory tool handler — the code that executes the `create`, `str_replace`, and `insert` commands — run every proposed file body through a deterministic secret/PII detector before writing to S3, redacting or rejecting matches; this enforcement lives in the backend the team controls, so it holds regardless of what the model decides to write.
rationale_a: The memory tool is client-side: Claude emits the command but the team's own handler performs the persistence, so a deterministic check in that handler is the only layer that can guarantee secrets/PII never reach the store — programmatic enforcement in code you control, not model goodwill, is what makes the invariant hold under adversarial input.
choice_b: Add a firmly worded instruction to the analyst agent's system prompt — "Never write API keys, passwords, tokens, or customer PII into memory files; redact them first" — and rely on the model to scrub each value before it issues a `create` or `str_replace`.
rationale_b: This correctly identifies the right content to keep out and the right commands to watch, but the flaw is objective: a system-prompt instruction is advisory, not enforced — the model can be steered past it by injected text in a scraped page, so it cannot guarantee secrets stay out of the persisted store. The guarantee requires a deterministic check in the client-side handler that actually writes the file.
choice_c: Switch the agent to summarized extended thinking (`thinking: {type: "adaptive", display: "summarized"}`) so a reviewer can read the model's reasoning and catch any moment it decides to persist a secret before the write happens.
rationale_c: Thinking display only controls whether reasoning summaries are surfaced; it does not gate or inspect the actual create/str_replace payload, happens too late, and requires a human in the loop — it provides visibility, not the deterministic guarantee that secrets never reach the persisted store.
choice_d: Enable context editing with `clear_tool_uses_20250919` so old memory tool results are cleared from the conversation, ensuring previously-seen secrets are dropped from the agent's context before they can be written to disk.
rationale_d: Context editing prunes stale tool results from the in-flight transcript to manage the context window; it does nothing to inspect or redact what the agent writes to the memory backend, so secrets and PII still flow straight into the persisted store.

stem: A team at a fictional firm, Larkspur Claims, is building a Claude Opus 4.8 pipeline to extract structured records from roughly 40,000 scanned insurance documents per night. Each extracted record must conform exactly to an `extract_claim` tool whose input_schema has six fields (claimant_name, policy_number, loss_date, amount_usd, adjuster_id, notes), of which the first four are mandatory and the last two are optional. During a pilot they observed Claude occasionally emitting extra, hallucinated keys (e.g. `confidence`, `source_page`) that broke their downstream Postgres loader, and they also saw records missing `amount_usd` entirely. They have decided to turn on strict schema enforcement so that every tool call is guaranteed to match the schema's shape. Which configuration correctly enforces the schema and eliminates both the extra-key problem and the missing-mandatory-field problem?
anti_pattern: Treating strict structured output as a single boolean flag while neglecting the schema-level constraints it actually requires (additionalProperties:false plus a complete required array), or attaching the flag to the wrong object (tool_choice instead of the tool definition).
choice_a: Leave the tool definition unchanged and instead set `tool_choice: {type: "tool", name: "extract_claim", strict: true}`, since strict enforcement is a property of how the tool is selected on each request.
rationale_a: strict is a property of the tool definition (sibling of name/description/input_schema), not of tool_choice; placing it on tool_choice does not enable schema enforcement and would not constrain the emitted keys or required fields. tool_choice only governs which tool must be used, not the shape of its output.
choice_b: Keep the schema as-is and instead add `output_config.format` with a json_schema plus an assistant-turn prefill seeding `{"claimant_name":` to force Claude to start emitting only the allowed fields.
rationale_b: output_config.format governs JSON output of the final response, not the input shape of a tool call, and assistant-turn prefill is removed on Opus 4.8 and returns a 400, so this neither enforces the tool schema nor is even a valid request on this model.
choice_c: Set `strict: true` on the `extract_claim` tool definition itself (as a sibling of name/description/input_schema), and in that input_schema set `additionalProperties: false` and list claimant_name, policy_number, loss_date, and amount_usd in the `required` array.
rationale_c: strict:true belongs on the tool definition and only guarantees schema conformance when the schema is itself complete: additionalProperties:false forbids the hallucinated extra keys, and a correct required array forces the four mandatory fields to be present. This is exactly the combination the two observed failures demand.
choice_d: Set `strict: true` on the `extract_claim` tool definition and add `additionalProperties: false` to its input_schema, but leave the `required` array empty so the optional `notes` and `adjuster_id` fields never block a tool call.
rationale_d: The flaw is the empty required array: with no fields enumerated as required, strict mode still permits a record that omits amount_usd, so the missing-mandatory-field failure they observed is not eliminated. additionalProperties:false correctly blocks extra keys, but the four mandatory fields must be listed in required to be enforced.

stem: You are the architect for "LedgerMint," an internal finance-ops agent at a mid-size firm. The recurring job: each weeknight, parse 40-some vendor invoice PDFs that land in `/srv/invoices/` on the company's own on-prem Linux host, normalize them with an in-house Python CLI (`invtool extract`) that depends on a license file and a Postgres connection string that exist only on that host, and write a reconciliation CSV back to a local NFS share. The vendor portal that produced the PDFs has a clean documented REST API and a published `invtool` command line; there is no requirement to click through any GUI. The agent loop runs on Anthropic's orchestration via the Messages API, and the firm's security team mandates that the invoice data and the Postgres credentials never leave their network. You must pick the tool that lets Claude drive this automation. Which choice is correct, and why?
anti_pattern: Reaching for computer use or the server-side code execution sandbox when the job's real constraints (a programmatic API/CLI exists; the data and credentials live only on the customer's own host) actually point to the client-side bash tool. The traps embody the failure to match the tool surface to where execution must physically happen and what the tool can reach: computer use for something that has a clean programmatic interface, and the server-hosted code-execution sandbox for work that needs the customer's local files, network, and secrets — which that sandbox cannot reach.
choice_a: Skip a dedicated execution tool entirely and have Claude return the shell commands and Python as text in its response, so an operator can copy-paste and run them on the on-prem host each night.
rationale_a: Emitting commands as plain text abandons the agent loop: there is no tool_use/tool_result cycle, so Claude never sees stdout, exit codes, or errors and cannot iterate or recover — it turns an automatable nightly job into a manual copy-paste ritual.
choice_b: Give Claude the server-side code execution tool (`{"type": "code_execution_20260120", "name": "code_execution"}`) so it can write and run Python to call `invtool extract` and process the PDFs in a managed sandbox you don't have to operate, then have it write the reconciliation CSV.
rationale_b: Code execution is fully server-side — the container runs on Anthropic's infrastructure and is sandboxed with no internet access, so it cannot reach the firm's on-prem `/srv/invoices/`, the host-local license file, the Postgres connection, or the NFS share, and routing the invoice data through it would violate the no-egress mandate. The flaw is concrete: the execution environment is the wrong side of the network boundary and is network-isolated, so the host-only dependencies are physically unreachable.
choice_c: Use computer use (the `computer` tool) so Claude can open the vendor portal in a browser, visually locate each invoice, and operate the `invtool` desktop workflow through screenshots and simulated mouse and keyboard actions.
rationale_c: Computer use is for driving GUIs that have no programmatic interface; here a documented REST API and a published CLI already exist, so screenshot-and-click automation is slower, far more brittle, and strictly unnecessary — the wrong surface for a job that is fully scriptable.
choice_d: Give Claude the client-side bash tool (declared as `{"type": "bash_20250124", "name": "bash"}`, schema-less), and run the tool-execution harness on the on-prem host so each `bash` command (running `invtool extract`, reading `/srv/invoices/`, writing the NFS share) executes locally where the files, license, Postgres connection, and network already are; the data and credentials never leave the firm's network.
rationale_d: The bash tool is client-side: Claude emits the command, but your own harness executes it — so it runs wherever you host that harness. Hosting it on-prem keeps all execution next to the local files, license, DB, and network, satisfying the no-egress mandate while still giving Claude broad programmatic leverage over the existing CLI and API.

stem: At Meridian Title, an invoice-processing agent built on Claude Sonnet 4.6 exposes a tool `extract_line_item` whose input schema has fields `vendor_id` (string), `amount_cents` (integer), and `memo` (string). The harness loop runs correctly: it continues while `stop_reason == "tool_use"` and returns each tool_result with its matching `tool_use_id`. To dispatch the tool, a junior engineer wrote a handler that takes the assistant message's serialized tool_use block and uses a regex like `"amount_cents":\s*(\d+)` against the raw JSON string to read the amount, and a similar pattern to grab `vendor_id`. In a batch of 4,200 invoices, roughly 3% produce a wrong `amount_cents` — and on spot-checking, the failures cluster on invoices where the `memo` field contains text like `"ref amount_cents: 1999"`, or where Claude emitted the fields in a different order. What is the correct way to read the tool arguments, and why does the current approach fail?
anti_pattern: Treating the serialized JSON of a tool_use.input as plain text and pulling out argument values via substring/regex matching, rather than deserializing it into a structured object. Raw string matching is brittle to key ordering, whitespace, Unicode escaping, nested objects, and values that legitimately contain the matched substring — it silently extracts wrong values rather than failing loudly.
choice_a: Set `disable_parallel_tool_use: true` so Claude can only emit one tool_use block per response; with a single tool call in the message, the regex will always match the correct `amount_cents` and the wrong-value problem disappears.
rationale_a: This conflates concurrency with parsing: disabling parallel tool use only limits how many tool_use blocks appear per turn and does nothing about substring collisions inside a single block's serialized input, so the wrong-value extraction persists.
choice_b: Keep reading the arguments from the serialized JSON string, but harden the regex with anchors and word boundaries (e.g., require the key at the start of an object and use `\b`) so that occurrences of `amount_cents` inside the `memo` text no longer match.
rationale_b: The objective flaw: it still parses structured JSON by string matching instead of deserializing with a JSON parser. A tightened regex cannot reliably account for nested objects, Unicode/escaped characters, arbitrary whitespace, and arbitrary key ordering, so it remains brittle and will silently extract wrong values on cases the patched pattern did not anticipate.
choice_c: Deserialize the tool_use block's `input` with a real JSON parser (e.g., `json.loads` / `JSON.parse`) into a structured object, then read `input["amount_cents"]` and `input["vendor_id"]` as typed fields. The regex approach fails because it treats structured JSON as flat text, so a substring like `amount_cents: 1999` inside the free-text `memo` field (or a different key ordering) matches the pattern and yields the wrong value.
rationale_c: Public Anthropic guidance is explicit: parse tool inputs with json.loads/JSON.parse and never raw-string-match the serialized input, because only structured deserialization respects key boundaries, ordering, nesting, and escaping — so a substring appearing inside another field can never be confused for the real argument.
choice_d: Add `strict: true` to the `extract_line_item` tool definition (with `additionalProperties: false` and `required`); strict schema enforcement guarantees the field values are read correctly by the harness, so the dispatch code does not need to change.
rationale_d: strict only constrains what arguments Claude is allowed to generate against the schema; it does not change how the engineer's handler reads them, so a regex over the serialized string can still grab a substring from `memo` and return the wrong amount.

stem: At Meridian Logistics, a staff engineer is using Claude Code to add a new pricing-tier feature to a 1,400-file monorepo. Before any code is written, the work requires understanding how 30+ scattered files wire together: the legacy billing module, three serializers, and a tangle of config loaders. A naive run would read all of these into the main session, and the engineer has noticed that once that raw file content lands in the conversation, the later implementation phase starts losing track of earlier decisions as the window fills. The team wants the main session to enter the implementation phase already knowing the wiring, while keeping the main context window lean so it can hold the actual edit plan and code. Which approach best satisfies these goals using Claude Code's documented capabilities?
anti_pattern: Treating /compact in the same session as equivalent to isolating verbose discovery in a separate subagent context. /compact is reactive and lossy summarization applied AFTER the verbose tool output has already entered (and polluted) the main context window, whereas an Explore subagent runs the discovery in its own isolated context and returns only a concise summary, so the raw output never touches the main window at all.
choice_a: Read all 30+ files directly in the main session to build the wiring picture, then run /compact to reduce the conversation back down before beginning the implementation phase.
rationale_a: The objective flaw: /compact is reactive and lossy and only runs AFTER the raw file contents have already entered and bloated the main window — it cannot prevent the pollution the way an isolated subagent context does, so it is not a substitute for proactively isolating the verbose work.
choice_b: Dispatch an Explore subagent to perform the multi-file discovery; it reads the 30+ files in its own isolated context and returns only a concise summary of the wiring to the main session, so the raw file contents never enter the main context window, which stays available for the edit plan and implementation.
rationale_b: A Claude Code subagent (e.g., Explore) runs verbose discovery in a separate context and returns a concise summary, preserving the main context window — exactly the documented mechanism for isolating verbose multi-file discovery so the main session enters implementation informed but lean.
choice_c: Define a custom MCP server exposing a read_files tool and call it from the main session, relying on the MCP connector to keep the file contents in the server rather than the conversation.
rationale_c: An MCP tool's results are still returned into the calling session's context as tool_results, so the verbose file contents would land in the main window anyway; MCP extends the tool set but does not provide the separate-context isolation that a subagent does.
choice_d: Increase the model's effort to xhigh in output_config so it can hold all 30+ files in working memory at once, then proceed to implementation in the same session without any subagent or summarization step.
rationale_d: Effort controls how much thinking/tool-calling the model does, not how context is partitioned or preserved; raising effort does nothing to keep raw file contents out of the main window and would still let discovery pollute the implementation phase.

stem: At Meridian Logistics, a 9-engineer platform team runs Claude Code across a large Go monorepo. They have three recurring frictions: (1) every engineer re-pastes the same 40-line block of project conventions (import ordering, the internal error-wrapping helper, the "no new top-level packages" rule) into the first prompt of every session; (2) roughly 1 in 6 sessions still produces a commit that skips the team's mandatory `make lint-fix` step before staging, despite the conventions saying to always run it; and (3) onboarding a new hire means hand-copying that convention block into their setup doc. The tech lead wants to both stop the repeated pasting AND guarantee that the lint step can never be skipped before a commit is created. Which approach correctly addresses both goals?
anti_pattern: Treating a CLAUDE.md (or per-prompt) instruction as if it deterministically enforces a rule. CLAUDE.md persists conventions so they need not be repeated each prompt, but its contents are still advisory context the model reads and can skip; only a hook gives programmatic, non-skippable enforcement.
choice_a: Move the 40-line convention block into a checked-in CLAUDE.md so it is loaded as persistent project context for every session (no re-pasting, and new hires get it from the repo), and separately add a PreToolUse hook on the git-commit action that runs `make lint-fix` and blocks the commit if it fails — because the lint guarantee requires deterministic enforcement that CLAUDE.md alone cannot provide.
rationale_a: CLAUDE.md persists conventions as project context so they need not be repeated each prompt and travel with the repo, while a hook provides programmatic, non-skippable enforcement of a critical rule; the two mechanisms solve the two distinct goals (stop pasting vs. guarantee a step).
choice_b: Put the conventions in CLAUDE.md, then raise the model's effort to xhigh and enable adaptive thinking so the model reasons more carefully and reliably remembers to run the lint step before every commit, treating the skipped-lint problem as a model-attention issue.
rationale_b: More thinking or higher effort can improve reasoning quality but never converts advisory guidance into a guarantee; a step that "can never be skipped" is an enforcement requirement that only a deterministic hook satisfies, regardless of effort level.
choice_c: Move the 40-line convention block into a checked-in CLAUDE.md, and within that same file add an explicit, strongly-worded instruction such as "ALWAYS run `make lint-fix` before any commit — this is mandatory and overrides default behavior," relying on CLAUDE.md's persistence to ensure the lint step is always executed before a commit.
rationale_c: The objective flaw: CLAUDE.md content is advisory context the model reads and can still skip, so it cannot guarantee the lint step — a deterministic guarantee requires a hook, not a CLAUDE.md instruction, no matter how forcefully it is phrased.
choice_d: Keep pasting the convention block but standardize it as a saved snippet, and have each engineer manually run `make lint-fix` and visually confirm the output before they ask Claude to commit, since a human-in-the-loop check is the most reliable way to prevent the lint step from being skipped.
rationale_d: This abandons CLAUDE.md's persistence (engineers still re-paste and new hires still hand-copy) and replaces a deterministic hook with a manual step that is exactly the 1-in-6 failure being observed, so it solves neither goal reliably.

stem: At a fintech startup, the platform team runs Claude Code in headless mode (`-p`) as part of an automated remediation pipeline across 47 microservice repos. A hard compliance requirement states that no commit may include a file matching `**/*.secret.json`, and an auditor has flagged that this rule MUST be enforced on every single commit with zero exceptions — a leaked secret is a reportable incident. An engineer proposes adding a strongly-worded, all-caps directive to each repo's `CLAUDE.md` ("IMPORTANT: NEVER commit any *.secret.json file; abort the commit if one is staged"), reasoning that CLAUDE.md is persistent project context that Claude reads on every session and that the directive is clear and unambiguous. The team lead is uneasy because the pipeline is fully autonomous with no human in the loop. Which approach correctly enforces this requirement?
anti_pattern: Encoding a non-negotiable, must-never-be-skipped rule as natural-language guidance (CLAUDE.md, a slash-command instruction, or a prompt) instead of a deterministic harness hook. Because the model interprets instructions probabilistically, an advisory rule will eventually be skipped or rationalized away; only a hook the harness executes can guarantee enforcement.
choice_a: Set tool_choice to {type:"none"} during the commit step so the model cannot call any tool, which prevents it from ever committing a forbidden file.
rationale_a: tool_choice:{type:"none"} forbids all tool use, which would block every commit including legitimate ones and stall the pipeline entirely; it is not a selective compliance gate and misunderstands what the control does.
choice_b: Configure a PreToolUse hook in settings.json that the Claude Code harness runs before the commit tool executes; the hook script deterministically inspects the staged files and blocks the action (non-zero exit) if any `*.secret.json` file is present, regardless of what the model decides.
rationale_b: Hooks provide deterministic, programmatic enforcement run by the harness, guaranteeing the rule fires on every commit regardless of the model's choice — the requirement for a zero-exception compliance gate.
choice_c: Lower the model to Haiku 4.5 for the pipeline and set effort to low, because a simpler, more constrained model is less likely to deviate from the explicit CLAUDE.md instruction and will follow the secret-file prohibition more literally.
rationale_c: Model choice and effort level tune speed, cost, and reasoning depth — they do not convert an advisory instruction into a guaranteed gate, and any model can still skip a probabilistic instruction, so this provides no real enforcement.
choice_d: Keep the rule in CLAUDE.md but escalate it from advisory wording to a mandatory project convention, since CLAUDE.md is persistent context loaded on every session and reliably overrides the model's default behavior, making a separate enforcement mechanism unnecessary.
rationale_d: The objective flaw is that CLAUDE.md content — no matter how emphatically phrased — is an advisory instruction the model can still skip or rationalize; it is not deterministically enforced by the harness, so it cannot satisfy a zero-exception requirement.

stem: A platform team at a fintech called Larkmoor runs Claude Code in headless mode (`claude -p`) as a CI/CD step that auto-applies dependency upgrades across 14 internal services and opens PRs. A hard compliance rule states that no commit may ever be pushed if the repo's `audit:secrets` scanner reports a finding — this is a blocking, zero-tolerance gate. During a pilot, an engineer wrote a detailed CLAUDE.md section instructing Claude to "always run audit:secrets and abort the push if it reports anything," and on 47 of 50 runs the agent obeyed. But on 3 runs the agent reasoned its way past the instruction and pushed anyway, twice because it judged a finding to be a false positive. The team needs the gate to hold on 100% of unattended runs. Which change best enforces the rule?
anti_pattern: Relying on a natural-language instruction in the prompt or CLAUDE.md to enforce a hard, non-negotiable CI gate (e.g. "never push if lint fails").
