Prompt instructions are advisory and can be skipped by the model on any given run; a critical, must-not-be-violated rule in an unattended pipeline needs a deterministic hook the harness executes, not a request the model may ignore.
choice_a: Keep the rule in CLAUDE.md but strengthen it — raise the model effort to `max`, add explicit "this is a zero-tolerance compliance gate, never override it even for suspected false positives" wording, and require the agent to print the scanner output before pushing.
rationale_a: This improves an advisory instruction but leaves enforcement in the model's hands; the objective flaw is that a CLAUDE.md/prompt rule is advisory and can still be skipped on some runs, so it cannot guarantee a 100% hard gate the way a deterministic hook can. Raising effort or wording does not change the enforcement mechanism.
choice_b: Add a step where, after the agent decides to push, a second prompt in the same session asks Claude to self-review whether the secrets gate was respected and to confirm "done" before the push proceeds.
rationale_b: Same-session self-review carries the same reasoning bias that already let the agent rationalize false positives, and confirming via natural-language "done" is not a reliable termination/enforcement signal; neither makes the gate deterministic.
choice_c: Move the gate out of the prompt and into a deterministic hook (e.g. a PreToolUse/pre-push hook in settings.json) that runs `audit:secrets` and blocks the push action itself when there is a finding, so the harness enforces it programmatically regardless of the model's reasoning.
rationale_c: A critical, must-never-be-violated rule in an unattended pipeline must be enforced deterministically by the harness via a hook; prompt/CLAUDE.md instructions are advisory and can be skipped by the model, which is exactly the 3-in-50 failure observed.
choice_d: Lower the risk by capping the headless run at a fixed maximum number of agent iterations, so the agent cannot loop long enough to talk itself into pushing past a finding.
rationale_d: An iteration cap is only a safety backstop against runaway loops; it has nothing to do with whether the secrets gate is honored, and a run can violate the gate well within any iteration budget.

stem: At Meridian Dataworks, a platform team uses Claude Code to add a feature that touches a sprawling 1,400-file monorepo. Almost every task begins the same way: Claude must trawl through dozens of modules, read long config files, and run repository-wide greps to locate the ~6 files that actually need editing — discovery output that routinely runs to tens of thousands of tokens and crowds out the actual implementation work. The lead engineer notices that by the time Claude starts writing code, the main session's context is already saturated with stale file dumps and search results, and answer quality visibly degrades. She wants an architecture where the verbose locate-and-orient phase does not consume the main context window that the implementation phase depends on, and where the loss of important details is minimized rather than merely deferred. Which approach best satisfies these goals?
anti_pattern: Treating /compact as the primary context-management strategy: letting a single main session accumulate verbose intermediate output (file dumps, grep results, build logs) and relying on reactive, lossy summarization to recover headroom, instead of structurally keeping that verbose work out of the main context in the first place via a subagent.
choice_a: Enable prompt caching with a cache_control breakpoint on the discovery output so the verbose file dumps and grep results are read back at ~0.1x cost, which keeps the implementation phase affordable despite the large context.
rationale_a: Prompt caching reduces the token cost of re-reading a stable prefix; it does not remove content from the context window or prevent the saturation and quality degradation caused by stale discovery output still occupying the window.
choice_b: Keep doing the discovery in the main session as before, but run /compact as soon as the locate-and-orient phase finishes and before implementation begins, so the search output is summarized away and the implementation phase starts with reclaimed headroom.
rationale_b: The objective flaw: /compact is a reactive, lossy summarization step — it runs only after the verbose output has already entered and saturated the main context, and it discards detail, so it cannot satisfy the stated requirement to keep that output out of the main window and to minimize (not merely defer) loss the way a subagent does.
choice_c: Run the verbose locate-and-orient phase in a dedicated Explore/general-purpose subagent that does the trawling, grepping, and file reading in its own isolated context, and returns only a concise summary (the ~6 relevant file paths plus the key facts) to the main session, which then does the implementation with a clean context window.
rationale_c: A subagent isolates verbose discovery in a separate context and returns a concise summary, so the polluting intermediate output never enters the main window — this is proactive context isolation, which preserves the main context and avoids lossy recovery rather than reacting after saturation.
choice_d: Raise the effort level to xhigh in output_config for the discovery phase so Claude reasons harder about which files matter, which will naturally shrink the amount of search output that lands in the main context window.
rationale_d: effort controls the amount of thinking, tool-calling, and cost the model spends — it does not isolate or remove verbose tool output from the main context, and higher effort typically increases tool calls and tokens rather than protecting the context window.

stem: A platform team at a fictional firm, Marisol Freight, runs Claude Code in a headless CI pipeline (`--print`) that auto-triages and fixes flaky integration tests on roughly 40 PRs a day. The agent legitimately needs to run the test suite, edit test files, and push fix branches, but the team has been badly burned once when an agent run executed `terraform destroy` against a staging environment while "cleaning up." Leadership's hard requirement is now absolute: the agent must NEVER be able to run any `terraform` command, and this must hold even on runs where the model misreads its instructions. An engineer proposes adding a clearly-worded line to the repo's CLAUDE.md — "Never run terraform commands under any circumstances" — and argues that since CLAUDE.md is loaded as persistent project context on every run, the rule will always be in force. Which approach actually satisfies the hard requirement?
anti_pattern: Relying on an advisory instruction (a CLAUDE.md convention or a prompt sentence) to STOP a dangerous tool action, when only a deterministic harness-level control (permission mode/deny rule or a PreToolUse hook) can actually block it. The model can skip a prompt instruction; it cannot skip a harness gate.
choice_a: Lower the agent to the Haiku 4.5 model for the CI pipeline, since a faster, cheaper model running with a tighter token budget is less likely to wander into running infrastructure commands like terraform.
rationale_a: Model choice trades off speed/cost/capability and does not gate tool actions at all; any model can still emit a terraform tool call, so swapping models provides no deterministic block and does not address the requirement.
choice_b: Keep the agent in its current permissive mode but add the explicit prohibition to CLAUDE.md ("Never run terraform commands"); because CLAUDE.md is persistent project context loaded on every run, it is reliably present in context and will therefore prevent the agent from ever issuing a terraform command.
rationale_b: The objective flaw: a CLAUDE.md instruction is ADVISORY — being reliably present in context is not the same as being enforced, and the model can still skip it. Public guidance is explicit that a prompt instruction can be skipped by the model, so it cannot satisfy an absolute "never, even on a misread" requirement; only a harness gate (permission mode/hook) can.
choice_c: Add a post-run verification step in a separate session that reviews the transcript after each run and flags any terraform commands the agent executed, so the team is alerted whenever the prohibition was violated.
rationale_c: Separate-session verification is good for catching issues after the fact, but it only detects a violation after the destructive command has already run; the requirement is to PREVENT execution, which needs a pre-execution gate (permission mode/hook), not after-the-fact review.
choice_d: Configure a harness-level gate: run the pipeline in a permission mode with a Bash deny rule for `terraform` (or a PreToolUse hook that programmatically rejects any tool call invoking terraform), so the action is blocked deterministically regardless of what the model decides. CLAUDE.md may document the rule, but the enforcement lives in the permission configuration, not the prose.
rationale_d: Permission modes gate which tool actions can run, and hooks provide deterministic/programmatic enforcement of a critical rule — both are enforced by the harness, so the block holds even if the model ignores or misreads guidance. This is the right tool for an absolute, hard-to-reverse-action requirement.

stem: At a 40-engineer fintech, the platform team standardizes Claude Code across the org. They add an internal MCP server named "ledger-tools" (which exposes a `post_journal_entry` action that writes to a staging accounting DB) by declaring it in the repo's checked-in `.claude/settings.json`, so every developer who clones the monorepo gets the same toolset. Compliance has one hard requirement: before ANY `post_journal_entry` call executes, a local validator script must run and the call must be blocked if the entry is unbalanced — this must hold on every run, even when a developer is moving fast, an unattended headless `claude -p` job runs in CI, or the model "forgets." A senior engineer proposes adding a strongly-worded rule to CLAUDE.md ("ALWAYS run `validate_entry.sh` and refuse to post if it fails") plus the same instruction in a saved memory, and argues that because it's repeated in two places the model will reliably comply. The team needs the configuration that actually guarantees the gate. Which approach satisfies the compliance requirement?
anti_pattern: Treating a model-facing instruction (CLAUDE.md guidance, a memory/preference, or a prompt rule) as if it were a guaranteed, deterministic enforcement mechanism. Such instructions are advisory and can be skipped by the model on any given turn; only a harness-executed hook in settings.json runs deterministically regardless of what the model decides.
choice_a: Keep the MCP server in `.claude/settings.json` and put the enforcement in CLAUDE.md as a mandatory project convention — instructing the model to always run `validate_entry.sh` before `post_journal_entry` and refuse on failure — since CLAUDE.md is the canonical place for persistent, repo-wide rules that apply to every developer and to headless CI runs.
rationale_a: CLAUDE.md (like a prompt instruction or memory) is advisory — the model can skip it on any turn — so it cannot guarantee the gate runs on EVERY invocation; a critical, must-always-hold rule requires a deterministic harness-executed hook, not a model-facing instruction. Repeating the rule in CLAUDE.md plus memory does not make it deterministic.
choice_b: Remove the MCP server from the checked-in `.claude/settings.json` and instead document in the README that each developer should add `ledger-tools` to their personal settings only after manually running the validator, keeping the dangerous tool out of the shared config.
rationale_b: This abandons the standardized shared toolset and still leaves enforcement to humans remembering to validate; it provides no automated, deterministic gate and fails the requirement that the check hold on unattended headless CI runs.
choice_c: Set `tool_choice` to `{type:"tool", name:"validate_entry"}` in `.claude/settings.json` so the model is forced to call the validator first, then call `post_journal_entry` afterward.
rationale_c: tool_choice is a per-request Messages API parameter that constrains which tool the model may call next; it is not a settings.json field and cannot chain "validate then post" or block an action, so it provides no deterministic compliance gate.
choice_d: Keep the MCP server declared in `.claude/settings.json`, and in that same settings file add a PreToolUse hook matched to the `post_journal_entry` tool that runs `validate_entry.sh`; the hook is executed deterministically by the harness on every invocation (interactive or headless) and can block the call by returning a non-zero/deny result, independent of what the model decides to do.
rationale_d: Hooks in settings.json are run programmatically by the Claude Code harness, not by the model, so they enforce a critical rule deterministically and gate a hard-to-reverse action regardless of the model's choices — exactly what a "must hold on every run" compliance gate requires.

stem: At Meridian Robotics, the 14-engineer platform team runs the same five-step pre-PR ritual in Claude Code dozens of times a day: regenerate the OpenAPI client, run the focused contract tests, update the changelog fragment, lint the diff, and finally run a security-secrets scan that the org's compliance policy says must execute before every single push — no exceptions. Engineers currently paste a long multi-step prompt describing all five steps, but they paste it inconsistently and sometimes skip steps under deadline pressure. The lead wants to (a) package the recipe so any engineer can invoke it by name, and (b) guarantee the secrets scan actually runs on every push even if an engineer forgets to invoke the recipe or the model decides to skip that step. Which approach correctly satisfies BOTH goals?
anti_pattern: Conflating a packaged workflow (slash command / skill — an advisory prompt the model may deviate from) with a deterministic enforcement mechanism (a hook the harness runs regardless of model choices). A repeated multi-step recipe is well-served by a slash command, but any step that MUST run on every invocation cannot be guaranteed by prompt text alone.
choice_a: Package the five-step recipe as a custom slash command (skill) so it can be invoked by name and stops being re-pasted, and ADDITIONALLY enforce the secrets scan with a hook in settings.json, since the hook is run deterministically by the harness on the relevant event regardless of whether the model invokes the recipe or chooses to skip a step.
rationale_a: A slash command/skill packages a repeated multi-step workflow for invocation by name, but its steps are advisory prompt text the model can skip; a mandatory step must be enforced by a hook, which the harness executes deterministically rather than relying on model compliance. Using both satisfies goal (a) with the skill and goal (b) with the hook.
choice_b: Package the recipe as a slash command and raise the model's effort to xhigh so it reliably performs all five steps, relying on the higher reasoning budget to ensure the secrets scan is never skipped.
rationale_b: Higher effort increases thinking, tool-calls, and cost but is a model-behavior knob, not an enforcement mechanism; it cannot deterministically guarantee a mandatory step and does nothing when the command is not invoked at all.
choice_c: Skip the slash command entirely and instead write a single large CLAUDE.md section documenting all five steps including the secrets scan, since CLAUDE.md is persistent project context that is automatically applied and therefore guarantees the scan runs on every push.
rationale_c: CLAUDE.md provides persistent project conventions so they need not be repeated each prompt, but those conventions are still advisory context the model can ignore and they do not enforce execution on an event; they neither package an invokable workflow nor deterministically guarantee a step.
choice_d: Package all five steps as a custom slash command, and inside the command's prompt add a strongly-worded, capitalized instruction ("ALWAYS run the secrets scan last — NEVER skip it under any circumstance") so the secrets scan is guaranteed on every invocation.
rationale_d: A slash command's body is an advisory prompt instruction, which the model can skip — emphatic capitalized wording does not make it deterministic — so it cannot guarantee the mandatory secrets scan; that guarantee requires a hook. It also fails goal (b)'s "even if an engineer forgets to invoke the recipe" condition, since an uninvoked command runs nothing at all.

stem: At a fintech called Larkfield, a platform team runs Claude Code across an 1,800-file ledger-reconciliation service. Twice last sprint, a session began rewriting a shared currency-rounding module mid-investigation before anyone had reviewed the approach, and one of those edits shipped a subtle bug. The lead's first fix was to add a line to CLAUDE.md: "Always present and get my approval on a plan before changing any files." It worked some of the time, but on a long, multi-tool exploration the session still occasionally jumped straight into edits, and once it announced in its own reply that the plan was "finalized and approved" and proceeded to edit. The lead now wants a setup where, for these high-blast-radius sessions, file edits are genuinely impossible until a human signs off on the approach, and the sign-off is what actually unblocks editing. Which approach best meets that requirement?
anti_pattern: Relying on a natural-language instruction in CLAUDE.md to STOP the model from editing files before approval. A prompt instruction is advisory and can be skipped by the model; the gate on a hard-to-reverse action (writing files) must be enforced by a deterministic harness mechanism — plan mode (a permission mode) — whose exit is the human approving the plan.
choice_a: Add a CLAUDE.md rule that the session must stop and consider the plan complete once it has written the phrase "PLAN APPROVED" in its own response, treating that self-declared marker as the signal that it may begin editing.
rationale_a: This terminates the planning step by parsing the model's own natural-language output for a "done"/"approved" string, which is exactly the loop-termination-by-text anti-pattern; the model can emit the phrase on its own (as it already did), so there is no human gate and no real protection against premature edits.
choice_b: Run these sessions in plan mode, which is a permission mode that prevents the session from writing files while it explores and drafts an approach; editing is unblocked only when the human reviews the presented plan and explicitly approves it, so the human approval — not anything the model says — is the gate that permits edits.
rationale_b: Plan mode is a Claude Code permission mode, so the no-edit restriction is enforced deterministically by the harness rather than left to the model's discretion, and the human's explicit approval of the plan is what transitions the session into being allowed to edit — exactly the "edits impossible until a person signs off" property the lead wants.
choice_c: Strengthen the CLAUDE.md guidance into an explicit multi-step protocol ("(1) explore read-only, (2) present a numbered plan, (3) wait for my reply 'approved' before any edit") and rely on the model to follow this protocol on every high-blast-radius session.
rationale_c: The flaw is that CLAUDE.md instructions are advisory project conventions that the model can skip — they do not deterministically block file writes — so this cannot make edits genuinely impossible before sign-off, no matter how detailed the protocol; enforcing a hard-to-reverse action requires a harness-level gate (a permission mode), not a prompt.
choice_d: Cap each high-blast-radius session at a fixed number of turns and have the model present its plan only on the final allowed turn, so it cannot reach the editing phase until that turn limit forces it to wrap up.
rationale_d: A fixed iteration/turn cap is only a safety backstop and controls timing, not permissions — it neither blocks file writes during exploration nor introduces any human sign-off, so the model could still edit on any earlier turn and approval never enters the loop.

stem: At Meridian Logistics, a platform team runs a 1.4M-line monorepo through Claude Code. An architect is wiring up a recurring "dependency-impact audit": before any large refactor, an agent must trace every call site of a deprecated `LegacyRouteResolver` across ~340 packages, read the surrounding code to judge blast radius, and hand back a tight written summary (affected packages, risky call patterns, suggested migration order) that the architect will read in the MAIN session and then act on personally. The trace is deliberately verbose — it touches hundreds of files and produces a lot of intermediate grep/read output — and the team's hard rule is that this audit step must NOT modify any source files; the architect wants to keep the main context window clean for the decision-making that follows. Which Claude Code subagent should the audit step use, and why?
anti_pattern: Choosing a subagent by the verbosity of the work it does rather than by what the parent context actually needs back. The general-purpose subagent can both investigate AND modify files, so reaching for it when the task is read-only discovery (where Explore returns a concise summary and is constrained from editing) over-provisions capability and invites unwanted writes; conversely, Plan produces a plan but does not by itself execute a multi-step build.
choice_a: Don't use a subagent at all; run the trace directly in the main session and rely on /compact afterward to shrink the intermediate grep/read output before the decision step.
rationale_a: /compact is reactive and lossy and is not a substitute for proactively isolating verbose work in a subagent; running the trace in the main session pollutes the very context window the architect needs to keep clean, which is exactly what a discovery subagent exists to prevent.
choice_b: Use the Plan subagent: it will read the 340 packages, produce the migration order, and isolate that verbose work so the main session stays clean.
rationale_b: Plan is oriented toward producing an implementation plan, but the task is a read-only discovery-and-summarize audit whose deliverable is an impact summary the architect acts on personally; selecting Plan conflates "produce a plan artifact" with "trace and summarize call sites," and Plan is not the discovery agent for verbose cross-package reads.
choice_c: Use the Explore subagent: it runs the verbose discovery (grep/read across the 340 packages) in its own isolated context and returns only a concise summary to the main session, which both preserves the architect's main context window and matches the read-only, no-modification requirement of the audit step.
rationale_c: Subagents isolate verbose discovery in a separate context and return a concise summary, preserving the main context window; the Explore subagent is the discovery-and-summarize choice and fits the read-only, no-modification constraint of this audit step.
choice_d: Use the general-purpose subagent: it isolates the verbose trace in a separate context and returns a concise summary to the main session, preserving the main context window while handling the cross-package discovery.
rationale_d: The flaw is capability mismatch: the general-purpose subagent can also modify files, so it over-provisions for a step whose hard rule is "must NOT modify any source files" — Explore is the constrained read-only discovery agent that fits the stated requirement, even though both isolate context and return a summary.

stem: At a fintech startup, an engineer is using Claude Code to plan a refactor that touches 14 service modules spread across the repo. The plan step requires reading all 14 files (roughly 600–900 lines each) to understand current call patterns, but only a short structural digest of each file (its exported symbols and where it calls the others) is actually needed for the planning reasoning that follows. The team lead worries that reading all 14 files directly in the main session will crowd out the main context window before planning even begins, and notes that later steps in the same session must still reason coherently over the digest. The engineer proposes fanning out the 14 reads across parallel general-purpose subagents instead of reading them sequentially in the main context. Which statement best captures the primary architectural reason this fan-out helps, grounded in how Claude Code subagents work?
anti_pattern: Justifying parallel subagent fan-out by the wrong primary mechanism — attributing the win to raw wall-clock speed or to better summary prose, rather than to the architectural fact that each subagent's verbose file contents stay in its own isolated context and only a concise summary returns to the main thread, preserving the main context window.
choice_a: Fanning the reads out across parallel subagents is primarily worthwhile because running 14 reads concurrently finishes far faster than 14 sequential reads, and the wall-clock speedup is the main reason to prefer subagents over reading in the main context here.
rationale_a: The objective flaw is misattributing the primary benefit to wall-clock speed; the documented purpose of subagents is context isolation (verbose discovery stays out of the main context and only a summary returns), and the stem's stated concern is crowding out the main context window, not latency.
choice_b: Each subagent reads its assigned files inside its own isolated context, so the bulky raw file contents never enter the main session; only each subagent's concise digest returns to the main context, which preserves the main context window for the subsequent planning reasoning.
rationale_b: Public Anthropic guidance states that Claude Code subagents (Explore/Plan/general-purpose) isolate verbose discovery in a separate context and return a concise summary, preserving the main context window — which is exactly the constraint the team lead is worried about.
choice_c: The fan-out helps mainly because parallel subagents each get a fresh, larger context window than the main session, so collectively they can hold more file content than the main session ever could.
rationale_c: Subagents do not grant a larger per-context window than the main session; the benefit is that their verbose contents stay isolated and only a concise summary returns to main, not that they expand total capacity.
choice_d: Reading the files in the main context but calling /compact after each file is equivalent to the subagent fan-out, because /compact reclaims the same context space that the isolated subagent contexts would have saved.
rationale_d: /compact is reactive and lossy and is explicitly not a substitute for proactively isolating verbose work in a subagent; it cannot reliably preserve the main context the way an isolated subagent context does.

stem: At Northwind Robotics, a 14-person backend team uses Claude Code across a shared monorepo. They want every Claude Code session to consistently follow two team conventions: prefer the repo's existing `db_session()` helper over opening raw connections, and never repeat the long list of project style rules in each prompt. A senior engineer adds a `CLAUDE.md` at the repo root documenting these conventions and notes that "these instructions override Claude's default behavior." A teammate asks why the team puts this guidance in CLAUDE.md rather than pasting it into every prompt. What is the most accurate explanation of what CLAUDE.md does here?
anti_pattern: Treating a CLAUDE.md instruction as a hard, deterministic guarantee. CLAUDE.md conventions are advisory context the model is steered by but can skip; only a hook executed by the harness enforces a rule programmatically every time.
choice_a: CLAUDE.md changes the model's permission mode so that any tool action violating the conventions is auto-rejected before it runs.
rationale_a: This conflates CLAUDE.md with permission modes; CLAUDE.md is conventions context and does not gate or auto-reject tool actions, which is the role of permission modes or hooks.
choice_b: CLAUDE.md stores persistent project conventions that Claude Code automatically loads as context for the session, so the team gets consistent steering toward their conventions without re-pasting the rules in every prompt; the instructions are followed by the model but remain advisory.
rationale_b: CLAUDE.md is the documented mechanism for persistent project conventions, removing the need to repeat them each prompt; it steers the model's behavior as context but is advisory, not a deterministic guarantee.
choice_c: CLAUDE.md stores persistent project conventions Claude loads automatically, and because it says the instructions "override default behavior," the harness deterministically enforces them on every session so a raw connection can never be opened.
rationale_c: The objective flaw is the claim of deterministic harness enforcement: CLAUDE.md is advisory context the model can still skip, and a wording like "override default behavior" does not make it programmatically enforced. Deterministic enforcement requires a hook executed by the harness.
choice_d: CLAUDE.md is a slash-command definition file: Claude only applies the conventions when the engineer explicitly invokes a `/claude-md` command at the start of a session.
rationale_d: This is wrong about the mechanism; CLAUDE.md is auto-loaded persistent context, not a slash command that must be manually invoked to take effect.

stem: At a fintech called Larkfield, a platform team runs a headless Claude Code job (`claude -p`) in their CI pipeline to auto-patch flaky integration tests across 240 service repos. The job edits the test files, and the team wants the pipeline to merge the patch only when the change is genuinely green. In a recent run, the agent finished its turn with the text "All 18 integration tests now pass and the flakiness is resolved," and the pipeline merged on the strength of that sentence — but a nightly run later showed 3 of the 18 still failing intermittently. The lead wants to redesign the verification so the merge gate reflects what actually happened, while keeping the autonomous patch loop and respecting that the agent's own turn ended cleanly (`stop_reason` was `end_turn`). Which redesign best establishes that the change actually passes?
anti_pattern: Trusting the same agent's same-context natural-language assertion that a change "passes" instead of executing the test suite as a separate, observable verification step. Self-review in the same session inherits the reasoning bias that produced the change, and a model's prose claim of success is not evidence the tests ran or passed.
choice_a: Keep verification inside the same headless session: after editing, have the agent run the test command itself, then parse the agent's final natural-language summary for a phrase like "all tests pass" (and confirm `stop_reason` is `end_turn`) before merging.
rationale_a: This correctly insists the agent actually run the tests and correctly reads `stop_reason`, but its merge gate keys off parsing the agent's own natural-language summary in the same session — the exact failure mode that already shipped a broken patch. The objective flaw: the gate must read the test command's real exit code/output, not the model's prose, and same-session self-review inherits the patch's reasoning bias.
choice_b: Cap the patch loop at a fixed number of iterations and treat reaching the final iteration without the agent raising an error as the signal that the tests pass and the change is safe to merge.
rationale_b: An iteration cap is only a safety backstop, not a termination or verification signal; exhausting iterations without an error says nothing about whether the tests actually passed, and treating "no error raised" as success conflates a silent/empty outcome with a verified one.
choice_c: After the patch loop ends, run the test suite as an explicit, separate step and gate the merge on that command's real exit code and output — ideally invoked from a fresh verification context rather than the same session that wrote the patch — so the gate rests on an observed run, not the agent's prose.
rationale_c: Correct termination and verification rest on observable signals: a change is "verified" only by actually executing the tests and inspecting the real result, and verification in a separate context beats same-session self-review because it does not inherit the reasoning bias that produced the change. The agent's natural-language "all tests pass" is a claim, not evidence.
choice_d: Add a CLAUDE.md convention instructing the agent to always run the full test suite and never report success unless every test passes, and rely on that instruction to keep the merge gate honest.
rationale_d: A CLAUDE.md instruction is advisory — the model can skip or misapply it — so it cannot deterministically enforce a critical merge gate; enforcement of a hard, hard-to-reverse action belongs in a programmatic hook or an explicit pipeline step, not a prompt convention.

stem: You maintain a Claude Code workflow that triages failing CI runs for a 40-service monorepo. When the nightly pipeline fails, the relevant job emits a single 90,000-line combined log (test output, build noise, and a handful of real stack traces) that you must read to identify the root cause, after which you continue a multi-step fix-and-verify session in the same conversation that may span dozens more tool calls. You have noticed that once the full log is in the conversation, the main session degrades: later planning and edits get sloppy and the context window fills quickly. You want a design that keeps the main session's context window clean for the long fix-and-verify work while still extracting the root-cause signal from the giant log, and you are comfortable spending a little more wall-clock time or money to get it. Which approach best fits documented Claude Code guidance?
anti_pattern: Treating /compact as interchangeable with subagent delegation: pulling a huge, noisy artifact into the main session and then compacting it, instead of doing the verbose analysis in an isolated context that only returns a concise summary. /compact is reactive and lossy, so the main window is still polluted before the summary exists, and detail needed later may be irreversibly dropped.
choice_a: Delegate the log analysis to a subagent: have it read and parse the 90,000-line log in its own isolated context and return only a concise root-cause summary (failing test names, the key stack traces, suspected files) to the main session, which then carries on with the fix-and-verify work. The subagent can run a cheaper, faster model since the sub-task is bounded.
rationale_a: A subagent isolates verbose discovery in a separate context and returns a concise summary, so the noisy 90K-line log never enters the main window — preserving it for the long fix-and-verify session; running the bounded sub-task on a cheaper model is an endorsed cost optimization.
choice_b: Keep everything in the main session but raise the effort setting to xhigh for the duration, so the model allocates more thinking and tool-call budget to absorb the full 90,000-line log and the subsequent fix steps without degrading.
rationale_b: Effort controls how much thinking/tool-calling/cost the model spends, not how much raw content occupies the context window; raising effort does nothing to keep the 90K-line log out of the main session and does not address window pollution.
choice_c: Read the entire 90,000-line log into the main session, then run /compact to shrink the conversation down to a root-cause summary before starting the fix-and-verify work, relying on compaction to recover the context window each time it fills.
rationale_c: The objective flaw: /compact is reactive and lossy and is explicitly NOT a substitute for proactively isolating verbose work in a subagent — the full log still pollutes the main window before compaction runs, and lossy summarization can irreversibly drop details the later fix steps need.
choice_d: Split the 90,000-line log into chunks and paste each chunk into the main session as separate user messages, letting prompt caching deduplicate the repeated content so the log effectively costs little context.
rationale_d: Prompt caching reduces token cost for re-sent stable prefixes but does not free up the context window — the cached content still occupies the model's window — so chunking the log into the main session still pollutes it and does not isolate the verbose work.

stem: Northwind Tackle runs a nightly "dependency-hygiene" job at 02:00 on a self-hosted CI runner: for each of 23 internal service repos, Claude Code should bump minor dependency versions, run the test suite, and open a PR only if tests pass. There is no human awake to approve anything, and the team has one hard, non-negotiable rule: the job must NEVER run `git push --force` against any protected branch, even if the model decides it would be convenient. An engineer's first draft launches the normal interactive `claude` binary from the cron script and writes "Never force-push to protected branches" as a line in each repo's CLAUDE.md, reasoning that CLAUDE.md captures durable project conventions. The job mostly works in manual testing but occasionally stalls overnight, and in one incident a repo's protected branch was force-pushed anyway. The team asks you to redesign the invocation so it is correct for an unattended runner AND so the force-push prohibition is actually guaranteed. Which approach is correct?
anti_pattern: Relying on interactive Claude Code (which expects a human at the terminal to approve actions) for an unattended job, and/or trying to enforce a hard, must-never-skip rule with a prompt instruction in CLAUDE.md instead of a deterministic hook. Prompt instructions are advisory and can be skipped by the model; only a hook enforces a rule programmatically.
choice_a: Invoke Claude Code in headless mode (`claude -p`/`--print`) so it runs unattended in CI/CD, and keep the "Never force-push to protected branches" rule in each repo's CLAUDE.md, since CLAUDE.md is the canonical place to record persistent project conventions and the model will follow it on every run.
rationale_a: CLAUDE.md instructions are advisory and can be skipped by the model, so they cannot guarantee a hard prohibition; only a hook enforces a critical rule deterministically. The headless-mode half is correct, which makes this almost right.
choice_b: Invoke Claude Code in headless mode (`claude -p`/`--print`), which is the documented way to drive Claude Code in CI/CD without a human at the terminal, and enforce the force-push prohibition with a hook that programmatically blocks the matching command, since hooks deterministically enforce a critical rule whereas a CLAUDE.md line is only advisory.
rationale_b: Headless mode (`-p`/`--print`) is the documented mechanism for driving Claude Code in CI/CD, and a hook provides deterministic/programmatic enforcement of a must-never-skip rule, which a prompt-level instruction cannot guarantee.
choice_c: Run the interactive binary but rely on the model's self-reported confidence: instruct it in the prompt to escalate and pause whenever it is less than 95% confident a git operation is safe, so a risky force-push is held for human review the next morning.
rationale_c: This keeps the wrong (interactive) mode for an unattended job and escalates on a self-reported confidence score rather than on task complexity or an explicit policy gap; confidence-based gating is unreliable and still cannot deterministically block the force-push.
choice_d: Keep launching the interactive `claude` binary from cron but add a fixed iteration cap (e.g., stop after 40 tool calls) so the job cannot stall overnight, and document the force-push prohibition in CLAUDE.md so the convention persists across runs.
rationale_d: Interactive Claude Code expects a human to approve actions, so it is the wrong mode for an unattended runner (it is what causes the overnight stalls); a fixed iteration cap is only a safety backstop, not a correct primary control, and CLAUDE.md still cannot guarantee the prohibition.

stem: At Meridian Robotics, a platform team runs Claude Code in their CI/CD pipeline (via `claude -p`) to triage flaky-test reports and open fix PRs across 47 microservice repos. Twice last quarter the agent committed directly to `main` and force-pushed before any human confirmed the change, because it followed a natural-language reading of the task. The team's CLAUDE.md already says in bold: "ALWAYS create a branch before committing, and NEVER run an outward-facing git action (push, PR, force-push) without explicit human confirmation." Despite this, the agent occasionally still pushes to `main` on its own. The team needs the branch-first and confirm-before-push rules to hold deterministically on every run, including unattended headless runs, without depending on the model choosing to comply. Which approach best guarantees this?
anti_pattern: Relying on a CLAUDE.md / system-prompt instruction to enforce a critical, hard-to-reverse rule. Prompt instructions are advisory and the model can skip them; deterministic enforcement of a critical rule requires a hook, which the harness runs programmatically.
choice_a: Add a PreToolUse hook in settings.json that intercepts the relevant Bash git invocations: block any `git commit`/`git push` while on `main` (forcing a branch first), and block `git push`/force-push/PR-creating commands unless an explicit confirmation signal is present, since the harness runs hooks programmatically and enforces the rule regardless of what the model decides.
rationale_a: Hooks provide deterministic, programmatic enforcement of a critical rule by the harness, whereas a prompt instruction is only advisory and can be skipped by the model — exactly the failure the team is seeing.
choice_b: Keep the rules in CLAUDE.md but make them far more emphatic and specific — add an enumerated checklist ("1. branch 2. commit 3. ask before push"), repeat the prohibition in the per-task `-p` prompt, and instruct the agent to self-review its git plan in the same session before acting.
rationale_b: The objective flaw: CLAUDE.md and prompt text are advisory only, so no amount of emphasis or same-session self-review makes them deterministic — the model can still skip them, which is precisely why a hook is required for a critical, hard-to-reverse rule.
choice_c: Lower the model's effort to `low` and set `disable_parallel_tool_use: true` so the agent runs git commands one at a time, giving it fewer chances to push to `main` accidentally in a burst of parallel tool calls.
rationale_c: Effort level and parallel-tool settings change how much the model thinks and whether tools run concurrently, but neither blocks a specific destructive git action — the agent can still choose to push to main on a single serialized call.
choice_d: Switch the CI job to a managed Agent created once via the API with the git rules baked into its system prompt and a gradeable rubric, then reference that Agent by ID from every Session so the constraints are centralized and reused.
rationale_d: Centralizing constraints in an Agent's system prompt is still an advisory instruction, not deterministic enforcement; a rubric grades outcomes after the fact and does not prevent the force-push from happening in the first place.

stem: At Larkfield Robotics, a platform team has standardized on Claude Code for their 280-service monorepo and written a CLAUDE.md convention that says "prefer subagents to keep the main context clean." A senior engineer is mid-task in a long-running main session — already 40% through its context window after exploring a sprawling auth refactor — and now needs to confirm one fact: the exact value of the DEFAULT_TIMEOUT_MS constant declared on a known line of a single config file. A teammate argues this is exactly the kind of read that should go to a general-purpose subagent, citing the CLAUDE.md rule and the principle that subagents preserve the main context window. The engineer wants the choice that correctly applies Anthropic's guidance on when subagent isolation actually pays off, given the rest of the auth refactor still has to happen in this same session.
anti_pattern: Reflexively delegating to a subagent for work that produces no verbose intermediate context, treating "spawn a subagent" as a universal good rather than a tool whose payoff is context isolation of noisy discovery. A one-line read returns almost nothing to isolate, so the subagent adds a round-trip and a separate context for zero context-window savings.
choice_a: Run /compact first to reclaim context, then read the constant in the main session, because at 40% usage the session is approaching the point where context must be reclaimed before any further reads.
rationale_a: /compact is a reactive, lossy summarization step, not a routine precondition for a trivial read, and 40% usage is nowhere near requiring reclamation; compacting here would discard real refactor context to make room for a one-line read that costs almost nothing.
choice_b: Delegate the read to a general-purpose subagent so the main context stays clean, since the CLAUDE.md convention says to prefer subagents and subagents are the documented mechanism for preserving the main context window.
rationale_b: It correctly states that subagents preserve the main context window, but the objective flaw is that it applies that mechanism where there is no verbose discovery output to isolate — the read returns a single value — so the subagent yields zero context savings while adding overhead, and treating "prefer subagents" as unconditional misreads guidance that ties the benefit to isolating noisy discovery.
choice_c: Have the main agent read the constant directly in the current session, because the value of subagent isolation comes from keeping verbose discovery output out of the main context — a single known-location one-line read produces almost nothing to isolate, so delegating it adds a round-trip and a separate context with no context-window benefit.
rationale_c: A subagent's purpose is to isolate verbose, noisy discovery (e.g., searching across the 280-service tree) and return a concise summary, preserving the main window; a one-line read at a known location has no verbose intermediate output to isolate, so direct reading is both cheaper and keeps the constant available in the main context where the refactor continues.
choice_d: Add a dedicated read-only "get-constant" tool to the agent's tool set so this kind of lookup is gated and parallel-safe, then call it from the main session.
rationale_d: Promoting an action to a dedicated tool is warranted for gating hard-to-reverse actions, staleness checks, or custom UI — none of which apply to a safe one-line read — and adding tools toward the ~18+ range degrades tool selection, so a bespoke tool is unjustified overhead.

stem: At Brightfold Labs, a 9-person platform team uses Claude Code across a TypeScript monorepo where every committed file must pass Prettier formatting and an ESLint check. They added a line to CLAUDE.md reading "After editing any file, always run `npm run format` and `npm run lint:fix` on it before finishing." Over the next three weeks, roughly one in six Claude Code sessions still produced commits that failed the CI formatting gate, usually on large multi-file refactors where the model edited 20+ files and skipped the formatting step on a few of them. The team lead wants a setup where formatting and lint-fixing run on every single file edit without depending on the model choosing to do it, and without bloating each prompt. Which approach best meets this requirement?
anti_pattern: Relying on a natural-language instruction (CLAUDE.md or a per-prompt reminder) to enforce a rule that must run deterministically on every occurrence. Prompt instructions are advisory and the model can skip them; only a hook guarantees the action fires every time.
choice_a: Move the instruction out of CLAUDE.md and into a custom slash command (skill) that runs `npm run format` and `npm run lint:fix`, and instruct the team to invoke that command at the end of every editing session.
rationale_a: A slash command packages the workflow but still requires someone — the model or the user — to choose to invoke it, so it remains advisory rather than deterministic; the objective flaw is that it does not fire automatically on every edit, leaving the same "skipped on a few files" gap. Hooks, not slash commands, are the mechanism for automatic per-event enforcement.
choice_b: Strengthen the CLAUDE.md wording with capitalized "IMPORTANT: you MUST format and lint EVERY file you touch" and repeat the same reminder at the start of every prompt so the model is far less likely to forget.
rationale_b: This stays entirely within advisory prompt instructions, which the model can still skip — emphasis and repetition reduce but do not eliminate the gap, and repeating it each prompt also bloats the context the team wanted to avoid. It does not provide the deterministic guarantee the requirement demands.
choice_c: Configure a PostToolUse hook in settings.json that matches the Edit/Write tools and runs `npm run format` and `npm run lint:fix` on the edited file; the harness executes the hook deterministically after each edit, independent of whether the model "remembers" to do so.
rationale_c: Hooks are programmatic, harness-executed enforcement of a rule that must fire on every occurrence, whereas a prompt/CLAUDE.md instruction is advisory and can be skipped by the model — exactly the failure the team observed. A PostToolUse hook keyed to the edit tools guarantees formatting runs on each file regardless of model behavior.
choice_d: Switch the editing sessions to Claude Opus 4.8 at effort "max" so the model has enough reasoning budget to reliably remember the formatting step on large multi-file refactors.
rationale_d: Raising model capability or effort cannot convert an advisory instruction into a guaranteed action; the requirement is deterministic enforcement on every edit, which is a harness/hook concern, not a function of model intelligence or thinking budget.

stem: At Meridian Claims, a data-extraction service parses ~14,000 scanned insurance forms per day, emitting one record per form with exactly the fields invoice_id (string), total_cents (integer), and line_items (array of objects). The team runs claude-opus-4-8 for the hardest, low-quality scans and needs the model's output to be reliably parseable JSON matching that exact shape so a downstream loader can ingest it without defensive try/except wrappers. An engineer proposes porting a snippet from an old internal runbook that seeds the assistant turn with a prefilled opening `{` to "lock the model into JSON," and pairs it with a system instruction describing the fields. During a staging rollout the team starts seeing intermittent HTTP 400 responses from the API on exactly the requests that use that snippet. Which approach correctly guarantees the JSON shape on this model generation?
anti_pattern: Reaching for the classic assistant-turn prefill trick (seeding the assistant message with an opening `{`) to force JSON shape. This was a common community pattern on older Claude models, but it is removed on the Opus 4.6/4.7/4.8 and Fable 5 generation (returns a 400), and even where allowed it only nudges the opening token rather than validating the full structure against a schema. The robust, supported mechanism is output_config.format with a json_schema.
choice_a: Keep the system instruction but move the shape guarantee to the tool definition: define an extraction tool whose input_schema has the three fields, set strict:true on the tool, and force it with tool_choice:{type:"tool",name:...} so the model must emit schema-valid arguments — while leaving the prefilled opening `{` in place.
rationale_a: Forced strict tool use is itself a legitimate way to constrain a payload shape, but this option leaves the assistant-turn prefill in place; the 400 is caused by that prefill on this model generation, so bolting a strict tool on top does not stop the failing requests.
choice_b: Drop the assistant-turn prefill entirely and request structured output via output_config.format set to a json_schema that declares invoice_id, total_cents, and line_items (with additionalProperties:false and the required fields listed); the model then emits JSON conforming to that schema.
rationale_b: On the Opus 4.6/4.7/4.8 and Fable 5 generation, assistant-turn prefill is removed and returns a 400, which is exactly the staging symptom; the supported way to constrain output shape is output_config.format with a json_schema, which validates the whole structure rather than just nudging the first token.
choice_c: Leave the prefill in place and raise effort to "max" in output_config plus add a stronger system instruction ("respond ONLY with valid JSON"), trusting the higher reasoning budget to eliminate the malformed responses and the 400s.
rationale_c: Effort and natural-language instructions influence reasoning and tone but cannot validate output structure, and neither addresses the root cause: the 400 comes from the removed assistant-turn prefill, not from insufficient effort, so the failures continue.
choice_d: Keep the prefilled opening `{` but switch the request to the deprecated top-level output_format:"json" field so the API knows to expect JSON, and rely on the system instruction to enumerate the fields.
rationale_d: This is wrong on two counts: the top-level output_format field is deprecated in favor of output_config.format, and it retains the assistant-turn prefill that triggers the 400 on this model generation, so it neither fixes the error nor uses a supported shape mechanism.

stem: You are building a document-extraction pipeline at a fictional insurance firm, Meridian Claims, that ingests ~40,000 scanned PDFs per night. A single Claude tool, record_claim, accepts a structured object (claim_id string, loss_date string, line_items array, total_amount number). Downstream a Postgres writer rejects any payload with an extra/unknown field or a missing required field, and last week 1,300 rows failed because Claude occasionally emitted an unschemaed currency_note key and sometimes omitted total_amount. You want the API itself to guarantee that every record_claim call conforms exactly to the schema — no extra keys, no missing required fields — rather than validating and retrying after the fact. Which configuration achieves this guarantee?
anti_pattern: Confusing WHERE strict mode is configured and WHICH schema preconditions it requires: putting the strict flag on tool_choice instead of the tool definition, or enabling strict without the closed-schema requirements (additionalProperties:false + required) it depends on. The result is a 400 or silently non-guaranteed parameters, defeating the goal of guaranteed-valid tool inputs.
choice_a: Keep the schema open and rely on a system-prompt instruction ("never emit fields outside the schema; always include total_amount") plus higher effort, since instructing the model is the supported way to enforce parameter shape.
rationale_a: A natural-language instruction is advisory and can be skipped by the model — it provides no API-level guarantee, which is exactly the failure mode (stray/missing keys) the pipeline already hit; enforcement must be programmatic via strict mode.
choice_b: Set strict:true on the record_claim tool definition (as a sibling of name/description/input_schema), and make the input_schema a closed object: additionalProperties:false plus a required array listing claim_id, loss_date, line_items, and total_amount. Strict mode then enforces that generated parameters conform to that schema.
rationale_b: Strict structured tool use is enabled by strict:true on the tool definition itself and requires the schema to be closed (additionalProperties:false) with required populated; together these make the API guarantee conformant parameters, eliminating both the stray currency_note key and the missing total_amount.
choice_c: Pass tool_choice:{type:"tool", name:"record_claim", strict:true} so the model is forced to call record_claim under strict enforcement, and keep additionalProperties:false with all four fields in required on the schema.
rationale_c: The schema part is correct, but strict is a property of the TOOL definition (sibling of name/description/input_schema), not of tool_choice; tool_choice only controls whether/which tool is called. Placing strict on tool_choice does not enable schema enforcement (and is not a valid tool_choice field), so the guarantee is never activated.
choice_d: Leave the tools unchanged and instead add output_config.format with a json_schema (additionalProperties:false, required set), so the structured-output JSON schema constrains the record_claim parameters.
rationale_d: output_config.format constrains the model's final assistant TEXT/JSON output, not tool-call inputs; it does not govern the parameters Claude generates for a tool, so record_claim payloads remain unguaranteed.

stem: At Meridian Freight, an internal team routes 40,000 inbound logistics emails per week into exactly one of seven workflow buckets (e.g. CUSTOMS_HOLD, BILLING_DISPUTE, REROUTE_REQUEST). Their first prototype on Claude Opus 4.8 prompts the model to "reply with only the bucket name" as free text, then matches that text against the seven strings; roughly 3% of replies arrive with stray punctuation, an explanatory sentence, or an eighth invented category, breaking the downstream router. The architect wants a design where the model can only ever emit one of the seven exact labels, the value is machine-parseable, and the call stays cache-friendly across the run. The team also wants the guarantee enforced by the API contract rather than by post-hoc string cleanup. Which design best meets these requirements?
anti_pattern: Trying to pin model output to a fixed label set by coaxing free text or by misplacing the strict flag, rather than declaring an enum-typed tool input made authoritative with strict:true on the tool definition (plus additionalProperties:false + required) and parsing the result as JSON.
choice_a: Define a single classification tool whose input_schema has a `bucket` property typed as an enum of the seven exact labels, set `additionalProperties:false` and `required:["bucket"]`, and add `strict:true` to the tool definition (sibling of name/description/input_schema); force the call with `tool_choice:{type:"tool", name:...}`, then read the label by parsing the tool input JSON with json.loads from the matching tool_use block.
rationale_a: An enum-typed tool input with strict:true (which requires additionalProperties:false and required) makes the schema authoritative, so the model can only emit one of the seven labels, and parsing the tool input as JSON (never raw string matching) yields a machine-safe value — exactly what an API-enforced fixed-label classifier needs.
choice_b: Keep the free-text approach but prefill the assistant turn with an opening that begins the bucket name (e.g. start the assistant message at "BUCKET: ") so the model is steered to complete only one of the seven labels, then strip the prefix.
rationale_b: Assistant-turn prefill is removed on Opus 4.8 and returns a 400 error, so this design fails outright; it also still relies on post-hoc string stripping rather than an API-enforced label set.
choice_c: Use output_config.format with a json_schema whose only field is a free-form `bucket` string, and put the seven allowed labels in the system prompt instructions telling the model to choose one of them.
rationale_c: A free-string field plus instructions is advisory, not enforced — the model can still emit an out-of-set value — whereas an enum type is what actually constrains the output to the fixed label set; listing labels in prose does not give the API-level guarantee requested.
choice_d: Define the same classification tool with a `bucket` enum of the seven labels, `additionalProperties:false`, and `required:["bucket"]`, but enforce strictness by passing `tool_choice:{type:"tool", name:..., strict:true}` so the forced tool call is schema-constrained, then json.loads the tool input.
rationale_d: The objective flaw: strict:true belongs on the TOOL DEFINITION (sibling of name/description/input_schema), not on tool_choice; placing it on tool_choice is an unsupported field, so the enum is not enforced by the strict contract the team explicitly required.

stem: You are designing a high-volume invoice-extraction service on the Messages API using Claude Sonnet 4.6. Every request reuses the same 9,200-token system prompt (extraction rules plus a JSON schema for 41 fields) and the same sorted, frozen tool list, then asks Claude to extract structured fields from ONE invoice. Engineering wants to maximize prompt-cache hits across the ~120,000 invoices processed daily. The current prototype builds each request's system prompt by concatenating the frozen rules, then appending a line `Processing run started at {datetime.now()}` and the invoice's `document_id`, and places a single cache_control breakpoint at the end of that system block. Cache writes are happening but usage.cache_read_input_tokens is near zero. Which change correctly restructures the prompt so the stable prefix is cached and the volatile content comes last?
anti_pattern: Treating prompt caching as something you "turn on" with a breakpoint while ignoring that the cache is a PREFIX match: any byte that changes earlier in the tools→system→messages render order invalidates everything after it. The trap is placing volatile content (a per-document ID, a timestamp, the varying extraction target) high in the prefix — or interleaving it before the cached block — so the prefix changes every request and the cache is written but almost never read.
choice_a: Remove the datetime.now() line and the per-invoice document_id from the system prompt entirely; keep the frozen rules + schema as the system block with the cache_control breakpoint at its end, and move the document_id (and any run timestamp, if still needed) into the trailing user message that carries the invoice. The stable prefix (tools → system) is then byte-identical across requests and is read from cache; only the volatile per-invoice content lives after the breakpoint.
rationale_a: Caching is a prefix match over the tools→system→messages render order, and any byte change in the prefix invalidates everything after it; datetime.now() and a per-invoice document_id in the system prompt are classic silent invalidators that change the prefix every request, so the cache is written but never read. Moving stable content first and the volatile per-invoice content into the trailing user message makes the prefix byte-identical and produces high cache_read_input_tokens.
choice_b: Switch the model to Claude Haiku 4.5 for this workload and enable the 1-hour cache TTL (ttl:"1h") on the system block; the cheaper model plus the longer-lived cache will raise cache_read_input_tokens across the day's 120,000 invoices.
rationale_b: Neither change addresses the root cause: the prefix still changes every request because of datetime.now() and document_id, so a longer TTL caches an entry that is never reused, and switching models actually invalidates the cache (changing the model invalidates the whole cache) while doing nothing to stabilize the prefix.
choice_c: Leave the system prompt structure exactly as-is (rules + datetime.now() + document_id) but add three more cache_control breakpoints — one after the tools, one after the rules, and one after the document_id — using all four allowed breakpoints so more of the request is covered and at least the earlier segments get cache hits.
rationale_c: Adding breakpoints does not help when the issue is that volatile content (datetime.now(), document_id) sits inside the prefix: a breakpoint only marks where a cacheable prefix ends, and any byte change earlier still invalidates everything after it, so the per-request timestamp before later segments keeps them from being read regardless of how many of the four breakpoints are used.
choice_d: Keep the frozen rules + schema and the per-run `Processing run started at {datetime.now()}` line together in the system block (so each run's full context is cached as a unit), put the cache_control breakpoint at the end of that system block, and move only the document_id into the trailing user message. Within a single processing run the timestamp is constant, so the prefix stays stable for that run's invoices.
rationale_d: The objective flaw: leaving datetime.now() inside the cached system block still places a volatile value in the prefix. The reasoning that "the timestamp is constant within a run" is wrong because datetime.now() is evaluated per request, not once per run, so it changes on essentially every call and invalidates the prefix — the timestamp must be removed from the system prompt (or moved after the breakpoint), not merely grouped with the stable rules.

stem: Northwind Support runs a Claude Opus 4.8 customer-support agent. Every request sends the same 14 tool definitions (a ~3,800-token, name-sorted, byte-stable block) followed by an ~9,000-token system prompt holding the frozen support policy and macro library; only the trailing `messages` (the live ticket thread) vary per request. Traffic is continuous — thousands of tickets an hour — and the team wants the entire tools-plus-system prefix served from cache on every request after the first, using exactly one `cache_control` breakpoint. An engineer is about to add the single breakpoint and asks you where it must go so that both the tools and the system prompt are cached together. Assume nothing else in the prefix changes between requests.
anti_pattern: Placing the cache_control breakpoint on the wrong block relative to render order (tools → system → messages), so the intended stable prefix is only partially cached — caching the tools but leaving the system prompt uncached because system renders *after* the tools.
choice_a: Put the single `cache_control: {"type": "ephemeral"}` breakpoint on the last (14th) tool definition. Tools render first, so caching them captures the largest reusable block and the system prompt is automatically included since it sits right behind the tools.
rationale_a: The objective flaw: a breakpoint caches only the prefix *up to and including* that block, and `system` renders *after* `tools`. A breakpoint on the last tool caches the tool array but leaves the ~9,000-token system prompt uncached (re-processed at full price every request) — the system prompt is not "automatically included," it renders behind the breakpoint, not before it.
choice_b: Put the single breakpoint on the last block of the live ticket thread (the final `messages` entry), so the whole request — tools, system, and the current ticket — is cached as one prefix.
rationale_b: Wrong because the trailing messages vary per request, so a breakpoint there makes the cached prefix unique to each ticket; every request writes a fresh entry and none are ever read, defeating the goal of reusing the stable tools+system prefix.
choice_c: Put the single `cache_control: {"type": "ephemeral"}` breakpoint on the last block of the `system` prompt. Because render order is tools → system → messages, a breakpoint there caches everything before it — the entire tool array and the full system prompt — so each later request reads ~12,800 tokens from cache.
rationale_c: The cache key is the rendered prefix up to and including the breakpoint, and render order is tools → system → messages; a breakpoint on the last system block therefore covers both tools and system in a single entry, which is exactly the documented pattern for caching tools+system together.
choice_d: Put one breakpoint on the last tool definition and a second on the last system block. You need two breakpoints — one to close the tools tier and one to close the system tier — or the system prompt won't cache.
rationale_d: Wrong because a single breakpoint on the last system block already caches everything before it, including all tools; the task explicitly calls for exactly one breakpoint, and a second one is unnecessary to cache the tools+system prefix together.

stem: A support-automation team at a fictional company, Larkfield Logistics, runs a Claude Opus 4.8 agent that handles roughly 9,000 customer chats per day. To save cost they put their large, stable 14KB triage policy at the top of the system prompt with a cache_control:{type:"ephemeral"} breakpoint right after it, then append the customer's latest message as the user turn. After deploying, an engineer notices usage.cache_read_input_tokens is 0 on essentially every request and their bill barely changed. Reviewing the code, they find the system prompt is assembled as the 14KB policy text followed by the line "Current server time: " + datetime.now().isoformat(), and the cache_control breakpoint sits after that whole system block. What is the correct way to restore cache hits while keeping the per-request timestamp available to the model?
anti_pattern: Treating prompt caching as a switch you turn on (add cache_control / add more breakpoints) rather than a prefix-match contract: leaving a per-request volatile value (a timestamp) anywhere inside the cached prefix silently busts the entire cache on every call, and the "fix" must remove the volatility from the prefix, not just annotate around it.
choice_a: Move the datetime.now() timestamp out of the system prompt entirely and into the per-request user message (after the cached prefix), so the stable 14KB policy is the unchanging prefix and the cache_control breakpoint follows only stable content; verify the fix by confirming usage.cache_read_input_tokens is non-zero on the second and subsequent requests.
rationale_a: Prompt caching is a prefix match: any byte change before a breakpoint invalidates everything after it, so a datetime.now() value embedded in the cached prefix changes every request and guarantees a full cache miss. The fix is to keep only stable content before the breakpoint and push the volatile timestamp into the message turn (rendered last), then confirm with cache_read_input_tokens.
choice_b: Keep the timestamp in the system prompt but reorder the system block so the volatile "Current server time" line comes first and the 14KB stable policy comes after it, then place the cache_control breakpoint after the policy text.
rationale_b: This embodies the correct instinct (stable content must precede the breakpoint) but the objective flaw is that the changing timestamp now sits at the very start of the prefix, so the prefix's first bytes differ every request and the prefix match fails before it ever reaches the policy — the cache still misses 100% of the time.
choice_c: Switch the cache_control TTL from the default 5-minute ephemeral window to the 1-hour TTL (ttl:"1h"), since a longer cache lifetime will let the entries survive long enough to be reused across the 9,000 daily chats.
rationale_c: TTL only governs how long a cached prefix lives; it is irrelevant when the prefix itself changes every request, because a different prefix is never a hit regardless of how long the previous one is retained. The root cause is prefix invalidation by datetime.now(), not an expiring cache.
choice_d: Leave the timestamp where it is but add three more cache_control breakpoints (one after the policy, one after the timestamp, and one before the user turn) so that at least the 14KB policy segment before the timestamp gets cached on its own.
rationale_d: Adding breakpoints does not change that the timestamp is part of the prefix being matched; the answer misdiagnoses the cause as too-few breakpoints rather than a volatile value sitting inside the prefix, and most of the prefix (everything from the timestamp onward) still misses on every request. The real fix is removing the volatility, not adding annotations around it.

stem: At Meridian Claims, you run a high-volume invoice-extraction pipeline on Claude Sonnet 4.6 that processes about 40,000 documents a day. Every request shares the same 9,200-token instruction block (extraction schema, field definitions, formatting rules) which you mark with cache_control as a system-prompt prefix, and each invoice's raw OCR text is appended as the final user message. To make debugging easier, a teammate proposes folding a per-request "processed_at" UTC timestamp and the document's unique intake_id into the system prompt right after the instructions, arguing it keeps an audit trail visible at the top of every call. After deploying this, your usage.cache_read_input_tokens collapses to near zero and your token bill climbs sharply, even though extraction accuracy is unchanged. Which change best restores cache efficiency while preserving the audit data?
anti_pattern: Treating the system prompt as a convenient place to drop per-request volatile data (timestamps, document IDs, the document text itself). Because prompt caching is a prefix match, any byte that changes between requests invalidates the cache for everything after that byte — so volatile content placed in the frozen prefix silently destroys cache reuse, even though the call still returns correct results.
choice_a: Leave the timestamp and intake_id where they are in the system prompt (right after the instructions) but move the cache_control breakpoint to sit immediately AFTER those audit fields, so the cached prefix now spans the instructions plus the per-request timestamp and intake_id and you get one large cached block per call.
rationale_a: This extends the cached prefix to cover the volatile fields, but those fields change every request, so the cached prefix is byte-different on every call and the cache is invalidated each time — exactly the collapse you are trying to fix. Placing a breakpoint after volatile bytes caches nothing reusable; the volatile data must be removed from the prefix and placed last in the messages array.
choice_b: Switch the cache_control TTL from the default 5-minute ephemeral window to the 1-hour TTL, since the cache is expiring between requests and a longer-lived cache will absorb the per-request timestamp changes.
rationale_b: TTL only controls how long a cached prefix survives idle; it does nothing about prefix invalidation. A changing timestamp in the prefix invalidates the cache on every request regardless of TTL, so a 1-hour window cannot help.
choice_c: Remove the per-request timestamp and intake_id from the system prompt so the entire cached prefix (tools->system) is byte-identical across requests, and instead carry that volatile audit data in the per-request user message after the cache breakpoint; verify the fix by confirming usage.cache_read_input_tokens rises back toward the ~9,200-token prefix size.
rationale_c: Prompt caching is a prefix match: any byte change in the cached prefix invalidates everything after it, so a changing timestamp/ID in the system prompt destroys every cache hit. Moving volatile content into the message after the breakpoint keeps the prefix frozen and restores cache reads, confirmed via usage.cache_read_input_tokens.
choice_d: Downgrade the pipeline from Sonnet 4.6 to Haiku 4.5 to cut per-token cost, since extraction accuracy is already unchanged and the cheaper model offsets the lost cache savings.
rationale_d: Changing the model is irrelevant to the root cause and itself invalidates any cache; the volatile system-prompt data would still break caching on Haiku. It treats a symptom (cost) instead of fixing the broken prefix.

stem: At Meridian Claims, a data-extraction pipeline runs Claude Sonnet 4.6 over roughly 38,000 scanned insurance forms per night. The request prefix is heavily cache-optimized: a frozen system prompt followed by a deterministically sorted list of 6 tools, with a cache_control breakpoint after the tool block, and only the per-form image and a short instruction vary at the tail. For each form the team needs Claude to ALWAYS return its output by calling exactly one tool, extract_form_fields, and never to emit a free-text answer or call any of the other 5 tools. They also need the tool block's cache write to keep being reused (verified via usage.cache_read_input_tokens) so the per-form cost stays low. Which approach meets both requirements?
anti_pattern: Reaching for the wrong lever: editing or pruning the tool definitions (or misplacing strict/single-tool flags) to make the model emit one specific structured tool call, when tool_choice already pins the call deterministically and editing tools needlessly invalidates the prompt cache.
choice_a: Keep tool_choice at {"type":"auto"} but prepend a strong system-prompt instruction ("You MUST always call extract_form_fields and never reply in text") so the behavior is enforced by the prompt.
rationale_a: A prompt instruction is advisory — {type:auto} still lets the model choose free text or another tool, so it cannot deterministically guarantee the single forced call; editing the system prompt also changes the cached prefix and invalidates the breakpoint, defeating the cost requirement.
choice_b: Leave the tool definitions and their sort order byte-for-byte unchanged, and set tool_choice to {"type":"tool","name":"extract_form_fields"} on each per-form request. Forcing a named tool guarantees that exact single call, and because toggling tool_choice does not alter the tools or system prefix, the cached breakpoint after the tool block keeps producing cache_read hits.
rationale_b: tool_choice {type:tool,name:...} compels Claude to call exactly that one tool, satisfying the "always one specific call, never free text or another tool" requirement; and because tool_choice lives in the request controls rather than the cached tools+system prefix, changing it does not invalidate the cache, so cache_read hits continue.
choice_c: Delete the other 5 tools from the request so extract_form_fields is the only option, leave tool_choice at its default {"type":"auto"}, and set strict:true on tool_choice to force structured output.
rationale_c: This is wrong on three counts: editing the tool list changes the cached prefix and invalidates the cache (losing cache_read hits and raising cost); {type:auto} lets Claude still answer in free text instead of calling the tool; and strict:true belongs on the tool definition, not on tool_choice.
choice_d: Keep the tool prefix unchanged and set tool_choice to {"type":"any"} on each request so Claude is forced to call a tool rather than answer in free text; the cache prefix is untouched so cache_read hits continue.
rationale_d: The objective flaw: {type:any} forces the model to use SOME tool but does not pin which one, so on any given form Claude may call one of the other 5 tools — it fails the "exactly extract_form_fields and never another tool" requirement. Only {type:tool,name:"extract_form_fields"} pins the specific tool.

stem: A platform team at a fintech called Larkfield runs an internal code-review assistant that has been pinned to Claude Sonnet 4.5 for the past year. Their request template sets thinking with a fixed budget_tokens of 8000 so reviews of large diffs get consistent deep reasoning, and they want some reviews to think even harder than others depending on diff size.
