stem: Northwind Mutual runs a Claude-based pipeline that extracts structured fields from three document types submitted by policyholders: 8,200 typed claim forms, 1,400 itemized medical invoices, and 250 handwritten incident statements per month. The architecture team built a 1,000-document labeled gold set (sampled proportionally to live volume) and a grading rubric that checks each extracted field against the label, then reports one number: overall field-level accuracy, currently 94.1%, which clears their 90% launch gate. A claims adjuster escalates that handwritten incident statements are "almost always wrong," but the dashboard still shows 94.1% and the team cannot reconcile the complaint with the metric. The lead architect must decide how to change the evaluation so the launch decision reflects reality. Which change best addresses the problem?
anti_pattern: Collapsing evaluation results into a single headline accuracy number, which masks systematically low performance on a minority document type behind a high-volume majority that performs well.
choice_a: Raise the overall launch gate from 90% to 97% and expand the gold set from 1,000 to 5,000 proportionally sampled documents, so the higher bar and larger sample force the aggregate to reflect the handwritten failures.
rationale_a: A higher threshold and a larger proportional sample still produce one blended number dominated by the 9,600 typed-and-invoice documents, so handwritten failures stay diluted; tightening the gate without disaggregating does not reveal which type is failing and may even reject a launch for the wrong reason.
choice_b: Keep reporting a single accuracy number but switch the gold set from proportional sampling to equal sampling (roughly 333 documents per type) so each document type contributes equally to the aggregate, then continue gating the launch on that one re-weighted aggregate against the 90% threshold.
rationale_b: The objective flaw is that this still reports a single aggregate and gates on it; equalizing the sample only changes the weighting, so a catastrophic handwritten score (e.g., 30%) averaged with two ~98% types still yields ~75% and could pass-or-fail without ever revealing which type failed — the fix Anthropic prescribes is per-category reporting, not a re-weighted blend.
choice_c: Break the evaluation out and report field-level accuracy separately for each of the three document types (typed claim forms, medical invoices, handwritten statements), and gate the launch on each per-type number rather than on the single aggregate, since the handwritten type is a small fraction of volume and its failures are diluted in the blended 94.1%.
rationale_c: Public Anthropic agent-design guidance is to report per-document-type / per-category accuracy rather than a single aggregate, because an aggregate masks failures on a minority category; here the 250 handwritten docs (~2.5% of volume) can be near-zero accuracy while the blend stays at 94.1%, so only a per-type breakdown surfaces and gates the real failure.
choice_d: Move the per-field grading from the same evaluation session into a separate verification session run by a second Claude instance, so an unbiased grader recomputes the overall 94.1% accuracy without same-session reasoning bias.
rationale_d: Separate-session verification addresses self-review bias in grading, which is a real Anthropic principle, but it is orthogonal to this problem: re-grading in a fresh context still yields a single aggregate that masks the handwritten failures, so it does not make the launch decision reflect reality.

stem: Northwind Research runs a multi-agent literature-review system on Claude Opus 4.8 (1M-token context). For each query, an orchestrator currently loads all 1,400 indexed papers (~780K tokens) into the context window so the model "has everything available," then asks one focused question per turn. The corpus is identical across turns and changes only when a nightly ingest adds papers; the per-turn question (~200 tokens) is the only thing that varies. An architect notes that most queries actually touch fewer than 30 papers, that each turn still bills the full prefix, and that p95 latency has climbed as the window has grown. The team wants to cut both cost and latency without losing answer quality. Which redesign best reflects Anthropic's guidance on context economics?
anti_pattern: Treating prompt caching as a substitute for retrieval discipline: assuming that because a large, stable corpus can be cached, it is acceptable to keep the entire 1M-token window full every turn. The trap ignores that cache reads are still billed per token on every turn and that a saturated window carries latency and attention costs that caching does not remove.
choice_a: Keep loading all 1,400 papers but place the stable corpus first with a cache_control breakpoint and put the varying per-turn question last, so the ~780K-token prefix is served from prompt cache on every turn. Because cached input is far cheaper than fresh input, this makes keeping the entire corpus resident effectively free and resolves both the cost and latency concerns.
rationale_a: The cache ordering (stable corpus first, volatile question last) is correct, but the objective flaw is the claim that this makes the resident corpus "effectively free" and fixes latency: cache reads are still billed at roughly 0.1x per token on every turn for the entire prefix, and a saturated window's latency/attention cost is not removed by caching — so it discounts re-sending irrelevant context rather than avoiding it.
choice_b: Keep the full corpus loaded and rely on running /compact each turn to shrink the window back down after the model answers, so the context never stays saturated and per-turn cost drops accordingly.
rationale_b: /compact is reactive and lossy and is not a substitute for proactively isolating verbose work; it cannot reduce the input cost of a prefix that was already sent that turn, and summarizing the corpus risks dropping the very papers a later query needs.
choice_c: Stop pre-loading the full corpus. Have a retrieval/subagent step fetch only the papers relevant to each query (typically <30) into the main context, isolating the verbose search-and-selection work in a separate subagent context that returns a concise set of relevant passages. This keeps the main window small so per-turn input cost and latency scale with what the query actually needs rather than with the whole corpus.
rationale_c: Anthropic's guidance is to retrieve only what is needed rather than fill the window; Claude Code subagents isolate verbose discovery in a separate context and return a concise summary, preserving the main context window so cost and latency track the relevant subset, not the full corpus.
choice_d: Switch the orchestrator to Claude Haiku 4.5 so the cheaper, faster model absorbs the full 780K-token corpus each turn, trading model capability for lower per-token cost and latency on the large window.
rationale_d: Downgrading the model does not address the root cause — paying to carry irrelevant context — and Haiku 4.5's 200K context cannot hold a 780K-token corpus at all, so the load would not even fit; model downgrade is for simple high-volume work, not for masking a context-design problem.

stem: At Meridian Analytics, a platform engineer named Priya is wiring a new long-document ingestion service that must pass entire 600,000-token legal contracts to Claude in a single request, so she needs the most capable model and has to hard-code its exact API model identifier and confirm its context window before the on-call review at 4 PM. Her team standardized on Claude Opus 4.8 because it is the most capable tier, and the config file requires the literal string the Messages API accepts in the "model" field plus the documented maximum input context. A teammate left four candidate config snippets in the pull request, each pairing a model-ID string with a context-window claim. Priya needs to pick the one snippet that is correct on BOTH the identifier and the context window. Which snippet should she choose?
anti_pattern: Recalling the marketing name ("Opus 4.8") correctly but transcribing it into the wrong API identifier format (dotted vs. hyphenated), or pairing the right model with a stale/smaller context-window figure carried over from an earlier model generation.
choice_a: model = "claude-sonnet-4-6" with a 1M-token context window.
rationale_a: Sonnet 4.6 also has a 1M context window, but it is the speed/intelligence-balance tier, not the most capable model the team standardized on, so its identifier is the wrong choice for an Opus 4.8 config.
choice_b: model = "claude-opus-4-8" with a 200K-token context window.
rationale_b: The identifier is correct, but 200K is the wrong context window for Opus 4.8 (1M); 200K is the context size associated with the smaller/cheaper Haiku tier, and a 200K limit could not hold a 600K-token contract.
choice_c: model = "claude-opus-4.8" with a 1M-token (1,000,000) context window.
rationale_c: The context window is correct (1M tokens), but the identifier uses a dotted "4.8" instead of the API-required hyphenated form "claude-opus-4-8"; the Messages API will reject "claude-opus-4.8" as an unknown model. The objective flaw is the dot-versus-hyphen in the version segment of the model ID.
choice_d: model = "claude-opus-4-8" with a 1M-token (1,000,000) context window.
rationale_d: The public API identifier for Claude Opus 4.8 is the hyphenated string "claude-opus-4-8", and its documented context window is 1M tokens, which fits a 600K-token contract in a single request.

stem: You are hardening the agent loop for "Lattice," an internal research assistant at a 40-person logistics firm. The loop calls the Messages API with a mix of client-side tools and the server-side web_search tool, and your reviewer insists the dispatcher have an explicit, exhaustive branch for every value the response's `stop_reason` field can take — no catch-all `else` that silently swallows an unhandled case. A teammate drafts the switch and asks you to confirm it covers the complete set of `stop_reason` values a single Messages response can return, so that nothing the API emits falls through. Which option lists exactly that complete set, with each value handled the way public Anthropic guidance prescribes?
anti_pattern: Treating the agent loop's set of terminal/continuation signals as smaller than it really is — omitting a documented stop_reason (here pause_turn) so the harness has no branch for it and silently mishandles a resumable turn, or inventing non-existent values. Correct termination depends on enumerating the full stop_reason set the API can actually return.
choice_a: `end_turn`, `max_tokens`, `stop_sequence`, `tool_use`, and `refusal` — five values. Continue the loop on `tool_use`, treat `end_turn` as normal completion, and handle `max_tokens`, `stop_sequence`, and `refusal` as terminal cases that stop the loop.
rationale_a: The objective flaw is that this omits `pause_turn`, a documented stop_reason the server emits when a server-side tool (here web_search) hits its internal loop limit; with web_search enabled the loop will receive pause_turn and, lacking a branch, fail the reviewer's no-fall-through requirement and never resume the paused turn.
choice_b: `end_turn`, `max_tokens`, `stop_sequence`, `tool_use`, `pause_turn`, and `refusal` — six values. The loop continues on `tool_use` (execute tools, append results, re-call) and on `pause_turn` (re-send the user message plus the assistant content to resume the server-side tool loop, without appending a literal "continue"); it terminates on `end_turn`; and it handles `max_tokens`, `stop_sequence`, and `refusal` as distinct terminal/recoverable cases.
rationale_b: Public Anthropic guidance defines the full stop_reason set as exactly {end_turn, max_tokens, stop_sequence, tool_use, pause_turn, refusal}; correct loop termination comes from inspecting stop_reason and branching on every documented member, with pause_turn resumed by re-sending rather than by an appended "continue" message.
choice_c: `end_turn`, `tool_use`, and `max_tokens` — three values; stop on `end_turn`, loop on `tool_use`, and retry with a higher cap on `max_tokens`. Detect any other situation by parsing the assistant's text for words like "done" or "finished."
rationale_c: This both undercounts the set (dropping stop_sequence, pause_turn, and refusal) and reverts to natural-language parsing of the model's text to decide termination — the explicit anti-pattern, since correct termination must inspect the structured stop_reason field, not the prose.
choice_d: `end_turn`, `max_tokens`, `stop_sequence`, `tool_use`, `pause_turn`, `refusal`, and `content_filter` — seven values, where `content_filter` is the terminal signal that a safety classifier blocked the output.
rationale_d: `content_filter` is not a Claude Messages API stop_reason; safety declines surface as `refusal` (with `stop_details`), so adding a non-existent seventh value produces a dead branch and misrepresents how Anthropic signals a safety block.

stem: At Larkfield Logistics, an architect is wiring a Claude-powered shipment-status agent that has five tools available. For the first turn of every conversation, the team has a hard product requirement: Claude must always call exactly one of the available tools (it should never reply with plain prose on that turn), but the architect wants Claude itself to pick which of the five tools fits the user's request rather than hard-coding one. The architect is reviewing the Messages API request body and needs to set the tool_choice field correctly for this first turn. Which tool_choice value satisfies the requirement that the model is forced to use some tool while still choosing which one?
anti_pattern: Confusing the four tool_choice modes — especially conflating "model decides whether to use a tool" (auto) with "model must use some tool" (any), or mixing up which value forces a single named tool versus forbidding tools entirely.
choice_a: Set tool_choice to {type: "any"}, which forces Claude to use one of the available tools while letting the model decide which tool to call.
rationale_a: {type:"any"} requires the model to emit a tool_use block (it cannot answer with prose) but leaves the choice of which tool open to the model — exactly the stated requirement.
choice_b: Set tool_choice to {type: "tool", name: "get_shipment_status"} so the model is required to use a tool on the first turn.
rationale_b: {type:"tool",name:...} forces one specific named tool, which hard-codes the choice — directly contradicting the requirement that Claude pick which of the five tools to use.
choice_c: Set tool_choice to {type: "none"}, which makes the model select a tool on its own without being constrained to a specific one.
rationale_c: {type:"none"} prevents the model from using any tool at all, the opposite of forcing a tool call; the description misstates what "none" does.
choice_d: Set tool_choice to {type: "auto"}, which lets Claude decide whether to use a tool and which tool to use among the five.
rationale_d: The objective flaw: {type:"auto"} lets the model decide *whether* to use a tool at all, so it may reply with plain prose — it does not force a tool call, violating the "must call exactly one tool" requirement. It does correctly leave the tool choice to the model, which is what makes it a near miss.

stem: You are the architect for "LedgerLoom," an internal accounting-assistant agent built on the Claude Messages API. Each request reuses a large stable prefix: a 40 KB frozen system prompt plus a sorted toolset of 11 tool definitions, followed by a short per-user question that varies every time. Your team enabled prompt caching by adding cache_control to the end of the system block, and you confirmed cache hits via usage.cache_read_input_tokens during a load test where requests arrived every 30 to 60 seconds. During an overnight reconciliation batch, however, individual users sometimes pause for several minutes between turns, and you notice the cache write recurs (full-price prefix) instead of a cheap read. A teammate asks what governs how long the cached prefix survives between requests and whether anything can extend it. Which statement correctly describes the cache TTL behavior and the option available to you?
anti_pattern: The near-miss embodies the trap of conflating the cache-write cost multiplier with the TTL duration, or misremembering the default TTL — getting the right mechanism (cache_control with a ttl field) but stating a wrong, checkable value (the duration or the override key). It looks authoritative because the surrounding mechanism is correct.
choice_a: The default cache TTL is 1 hour, and the only shorter option is a 5-minute window set with ttl:"5m"; since your gaps are only a few minutes, the cache should already survive, so the recurring writes mean caching was never actually enabled.
rationale_a: This inverts the defaults — the default TTL is 5 minutes, not 1 hour — and misdiagnoses the symptom, since confirmed cache_read_input_tokens during the load test proves caching was enabled and the multi-minute gaps are exactly what expires the 5-minute window.
choice_b: The default cache TTL is 5 minutes (refreshed on each cache hit), so gaps longer than that force a fresh cache write; you can extend it by setting ttl:"1h" inside cache_control to get a 1-hour TTL, at a higher write cost (roughly 2x base versus roughly 1.25x for the 5-minute write).
rationale_b: Public Anthropic guidance states the ephemeral cache has a 5-minute default TTL with a 1-hour option available via ttl:"1h", and that the 1-hour write costs about 2x base versus about 1.25x for the 5-minute write — matching the multi-minute-gap symptom exactly.
choice_c: Cache TTL is fixed at 5 minutes and cannot be changed; to survive longer idle periods you must instead send a tiny keep-alive request to the same prefix before each timeout to refresh the cache, since no longer-lived TTL exists.
rationale_c: This is wrong because a 1-hour TTL is available via ttl:"1h"; the claim that the TTL cannot be changed and that a keep-alive hack is the only recourse contradicts the documented configurable TTL.
choice_d: The default cache TTL is 5 minutes, and you can extend it by setting ttl:"1h" inside cache_control for a 1-hour TTL; the 1-hour write is billed at the same roughly 1.25x base rate as the 5-minute write, so extending it has no additional write cost.
rationale_d: The mechanism (5-minute default, ttl:"1h" override key) is exactly right, but it states an objectively wrong price: the 1-hour cache write is approximately 2x base, not the 1.25x of the 5-minute write, so the claim of "no additional write cost" is false.

stem: You are architecting a long-running support agent at a fictional firm called Larkfield Logistics. The team wants to maximize prompt-cache hits by marking several stable prefix segments — the frozen system prompt, a sorted tool list, a static policy appendix, and a per-customer profile block — each with its own cache_control:{type:"ephemeral"} marker so the cache can be reused at multiple prefix boundaries. A junior engineer asks how many cache_control breakpoints the Messages API allows in a single request before they need to consolidate. What is the correct limit?
anti_pattern: The traps embody confusion about the fixed ceiling on cache_control breakpoints per request — substituting a plausible-but-wrong cap (an off-by-one) or conflating breakpoints with unrelated limits (TTL minutes, total cacheable tokens).
choice_a: There is no fixed limit on breakpoints; you may mark as many prefix segments as you have stable content.
rationale_a: This is false — the API enforces a hard ceiling of 4 cache_control breakpoints per request, so unlimited marking is not permitted.
choice_b: A single request may include at most 4 cache_control breakpoints.
rationale_b: The Messages API caps prompt caching at a maximum of 4 cache_control breakpoints per request; their four planned stable segments fit exactly within that limit.
choice_c: A single request may include at most 5 cache_control breakpoints.
rationale_c: This is off by one: the documented maximum is 4 breakpoints per request, not 5, so relying on a fifth marker would be rejected or ignored.
choice_d: The limit is 5 minutes, since each breakpoint expires under the default ephemeral TTL.
rationale_d: This conflates the breakpoint count with the cache TTL; 5 minutes is the default ephemeral cache lifetime, not a limit on how many breakpoints a request may contain.

stem: Northwind Analytics runs an overnight enrichment job that classifies roughly 240,000 archived support tickets with Claude Haiku 4.5. None of the results are needed in real time — the downstream dashboard refreshes once each morning — so the platform team wants to move the job off the synchronous Messages API and onto the Message Batches API to cut cost. A junior engineer is drafting the batching layer and asks you to confirm three things before they ship: how many requests can go in a single batch, what cost reduction batching provides versus synchronous calls, and how long the completed results stay retrievable so a failed morning ETL run can simply re-pull yesterday's output instead of re-submitting. Which statement correctly captures all three points for the Message Batches API?
anti_pattern: The near-miss and distractors embody the trap of half-remembering Message Batches facts: conflating the per-batch request-count ceiling with the byte-size ceiling, misstating how long results are retained, or claiming the discount requires a special parameter rather than being intrinsic to the endpoint. Each plausible answer pairs one true Batches fact with one specific wrong number or mechanism.
choice_a: A single batch may contain up to 100,000 requests (256 MB total), batching costs 50% less than synchronous Messages API calls, and completed results stay retrievable for 24 hours after the batch finishes — so the ETL must re-pull within that window or re-submit the batch.
rationale_a: The request count, size cap, and 50% discount are all correct, but the retention figure is wrong: results are kept for 29 days, not 24 hours. The 24-hour value is the maximum processing time (most batches finish under 1 hour), not the result-retention window — conflating the two is the objective flaw.
choice_b: A single batch may contain up to 100,000 requests (256 MB total) and results are retained for 29 days, but the 50% discount only applies when you set a discount_tier parameter on the batch request; otherwise batched calls are billed at the standard synchronous rate.
rationale_b: The 50% cost reduction is intrinsic to using the Message Batches API and requires no special parameter; there is no discount_tier flag, so conditioning the discount on it is fabricated.
choice_c: A single batch may contain up to 256,000 requests, batching costs 50% less than synchronous calls, and results are retained for 29 days; the per-batch limit is purely a request count with no separate byte-size cap.
rationale_c: This misstates the ceiling: a batch is limited to 100K requests AND 256 MB, not 256K requests, and the 256 MB byte cap is a real separate limit — so claiming there is no size cap is wrong.
choice_d: A single batch may contain up to 100,000 requests (and is also capped at 256 MB total), batching costs 50% less than the equivalent synchronous Messages API calls, and results remain available for retrieval for 29 days after the batch is created — so a failed ETL run can re-pull recent output without re-submitting.
rationale_d: Public Anthropic guidance states a batch holds up to 100K requests / 256 MB, costs 50% less than synchronous calls, and retains results for 29 days; all three values are stated correctly, so re-pulling yesterday's output is valid.

stem: Northwind Analytics submits a nightly Message Batches job of 12,000 document-summarization requests, each carrying a unique custom_id like "doc-4471". A junior engineer writes the result-collection code so that after polling processing_status until it reads "ended", it zips the returned results array against the original input array by index — result[0] paired with input[0], and so on — then writes each summary back to the matching database row. During testing the summaries are frequently attached to the wrong documents. A teammate asks how the code should match each batch result back to its originating request. Which approach is correct?
anti_pattern: Assuming the Batches API returns results in the same order they were submitted, so an engineer pairs each result with its input by array position instead of by the custom_id that travels with each request.
choice_a: Match each result to its request using the custom_id carried on the result, building a lookup keyed by custom_id, because batch results can arrive in any order and are not guaranteed to follow submission order.
rationale_a: Message Batches process asynchronously and results arrive in any order, so the only reliable join key is the custom_id that travels with each request and result — pairing by position is unsafe.
choice_b: Match by the tool_use_id returned on each result, building a lookup keyed by tool_use_id to associate each summary with its original document.
rationale_b: tool_use_id is the identifier that pairs a tool_use block with its tool_result within a single agent turn; it is unrelated to joining Batches API results, which are keyed by custom_id.
choice_c: Match each result to its request by its index position in the returned results array, but first re-sort the results array by custom_id so it lines up with a similarly sorted copy of the input array before zipping by position.
rationale_c: The objective flaw: it still ultimately joins by array position. Sorting both sides by custom_id is just a fragile way to reconstruct a key-based match — any missing, failed, or extra result throws off every subsequent index, whereas a direct custom_id lookup is robust. The documented mechanism is to key by custom_id, not to re-sort and zip.
choice_d: Match by submission order: the Batches API returns results in the exact order the requests were sent, so zipping the results array against the input array by index is the documented, reliable approach.
rationale_d: This is false — batch results arrive in any order, not submission order, which is precisely the bug producing the mismatched summaries.

stem: At a logistics startup, you are architecting a pre-flight budgeting layer for a support-summarization service that sends large ticket threads to Claude Opus 4.8. Before each call, the service must compute an accurate input-token count so it can reject any payload that would exceed a per-tenant 200K-token quota and so it can attribute spend precisely across roughly 40 tenants. A backend engineer proposes reusing the team's existing Python utility that wraps OpenAI's tiktoken library, arguing it is already battle-tested, runs locally with zero network latency, and "tokenizers are basically interchangeable." Accuracy of the count is the hard requirement here, since an undercount lets oversized requests through and a miscount corrupts billing. Which approach should you adopt to obtain the token count?
anti_pattern: Reaching for a familiar third-party tokenizer (tiktoken) or a hard-coded character-ratio heuristic to estimate Claude token counts, instead of the provider's own model-specific count_tokens endpoint. Both shortcuts produce systematically wrong counts because they don't model Claude's actual tokenizer.
choice_a: Call Anthropic's count_tokens endpoint for the specific model (Opus 4.8), passing the same messages/system/tools you intend to send, and use the returned count for both the quota check and billing attribution.
rationale_a: Token counts are tokenizer-specific, so accuracy requires Anthropic's own count_tokens endpoint scoped to the exact model; it reflects Claude's actual tokenization of the full request (messages, system, tools) rather than another vendor's scheme.
choice_b: Use Anthropic's count_tokens endpoint, but call it once for a representative model and reuse that single count across Opus 4.8, Sonnet 4.6, and Haiku 4.5 since they share the same tokenizer family.
rationale_b: The flaw is the assumption that one count_tokens call generalizes across models: the endpoint is model-specific, so a count must be requested per target model rather than computed once and reused across Opus, Sonnet, and Haiku.
choice_c: Skip a dedicated token API entirely and estimate the count locally with a fixed characters-per-token ratio (about 4 characters per token) applied to the serialized request body.
rationale_c: A fixed character-per-token heuristic is only a rough approximation and does not match Claude's actual tokenizer, so it fails the hard accuracy requirement for both quota gating and billing.
choice_d: Adopt the engineer's tiktoken-based utility for the count, since it is local, fast, and tokenizers across major LLM providers are close enough for quota enforcement and billing.
rationale_d: tiktoken is OpenAI's tokenizer and undercounts Claude tokens, so using it violates the accuracy requirement and would let oversized requests slip past the quota while corrupting spend attribution.

stem: You are an architect at a fictional fintech startup, Larkspur Ledger, configuring a nightly batch job that runs an unusually gnarly reconciliation reasoning task across 3,200 ledger entries. You want to dial the model's reasoning to the absolute maximum the API allows, so you plan to set the effort level in output_config to its highest value. A teammate asks which effort value represents the ceiling and whether the model you've selected can actually accept it. You are currently routing the job to Claude Opus 4.8. Which statement correctly describes the highest effort level and its tier support?
anti_pattern: Assuming every effort level is available on every model tier, when the highest effort level (max) is restricted to the Opus tier — conflating the full enum of effort values with the subset any given model actually accepts.
choice_a: There is no named ceiling; effort is set with a fixed budget_tokens integer, so you should pass the largest budget_tokens value the model allows.
rationale_a: Fixed budget_tokens is deprecated on 4.6 and removed (400) on 4.7/4.8; effort uses named levels (low..max) in output_config, so budget_tokens is the wrong and removed mechanism.
choice_b: The highest effort level is "max", but it lives in tool_choice rather than output_config, so you must set it there for Opus 4.8 to use it.
rationale_b: Effort is configured in output_config, not tool_choice — tool_choice controls whether/which tool is used (auto/any/tool/none), so placing effort there is the wrong mechanism.
choice_c: The highest effort level is "max", and it is supported on the Opus tier, so Claude Opus 4.8 can accept effort: "max" in output_config.
rationale_c: Public guidance lists effort ∈ low|medium|high|xhigh|max in output_config, with "max" being the Opus-tier ceiling — so routing to Opus 4.8 lets you set effort: "max".
choice_d: The highest effort level is "xhigh", and since it was added on 4.7 it is available on Opus 4.8, so you should set effort: "xhigh" as the maximum.
rationale_d: The objective flaw: "xhigh" is NOT the highest effort level — "max" sits above it. xhigh was indeed added on 4.7 and Opus 4.8 supports it, but it is not the ceiling, so this answer misnames the maximum.

stem: Aldergrove Compliance is migrating a long-lived contract-clause extraction agent from an older Claude generation onto claude-opus-4-8, where each run reasons over roughly 40 multi-page filings and must produce thorough, auditable analysis. Their existing request body still sets thinking to a fixed object of the form {"type":"enabled","budget_tokens":12000}, which worked on their previous model, and a platform engineer wants the migration to keep deep reasoning while staying within current API guidance. During a staging smoke test against Opus 4.8 the first request comes back as an HTTP 400 before any tokens are generated, and the team needs to know the correct way to express "let the model reason as much as the task warrants, with high reasoning effort." Which configuration change should they make?
anti_pattern: The trap is carrying forward the deprecated fixed-budget thinking mechanism (budget_tokens) that worked on earlier models, or attaching effort to the wrong config object. On Opus 4.7/4.8 the fixed budget_tokens form is removed and returns a 400; the supported form is adaptive thinking, and effort lives in output_config — so options that re-use the old budget field, or that mislocate effort, are objectively wrong even though they look like reasonable migrations.
choice_a: Keep the fixed thinking:{"type":"enabled","budget_tokens":12000} object but raise budget_tokens to 32000 so the larger explicit budget satisfies Opus 4.8's stricter reasoning requirements.
rationale_a: This misreads the 400 as a budget-too-small error; budget_tokens is removed entirely on Opus 4.8, so any value — large or small — is rejected, and increasing it does not address the actual cause.
choice_b: Switch to adaptive thinking with thinking:{"type":"adaptive"}, but keep controlling the amount of reasoning by setting budget_tokens:12000 alongside it so the deep-reasoning behavior is preserved during the migration.
rationale_b: Adaptive thinking is the correct mechanism, but the objective flaw is retaining budget_tokens: that field is deprecated on 4.6 and REMOVED on 4.7/4.8, so the request still returns a 400 — the amount of reasoning must be steered via the effort setting, not budget_tokens.
choice_c: Leave thinking unset and instead add disable_parallel_tool_use:true plus tool_choice:{"type":"any"}, since the 400 is caused by tool-configuration conflicts rather than the thinking object.
rationale_c: The 400 comes from the removed budget_tokens thinking form, not from tool settings; tool_choice and parallel-tool flags are unrelated to thinking configuration and do nothing to resolve the rejection.
choice_d: Replace the fixed-budget object with adaptive thinking — thinking:{"type":"adaptive"} — and set the reasoning effort to "high" inside output_config, since fixed budget_tokens is removed on Opus 4.8 (returns 400) and effort is the supported knob for how much the model thinks.
rationale_d: On Claude 4.7/4.8 fixed budget_tokens is removed and rejected with a 400, while adaptive thinking lets the model size its own reasoning; reasoning effort (low|medium|high|xhigh|max) is carried in output_config, so this is the only option that is both accepted by the API and expresses "reason as much as warranted, at high effort."

stem: You are wiring a remote MCP server named "ticket-search" into a customer-support assistant built directly on the Claude Messages API (no Claude Code, no managed Agents). The server is reachable over HTTPS and your team has added a single block to the request: mcp_servers=[{type:"url", url:"https://mcp.acme.example/sse", name:"ticket-search"}]. On the first test call the API returns a 400 error instead of letting Claude call the server's tools, and a teammate insists the model should "just discover the tools from the server block." Your lead asks you to state exactly what the request is missing so the connector works on the very next call. Which configuration correctly enables the MCP connector?
anti_pattern: Declaring the MCP server block alone and assuming Claude can use its tools — forgetting that the connector also requires a matching mcp_toolset entry in tools, so the server is registered but no tools are ever exposed (a 400).
choice_a: Add a tools entry of type "mcp_toolset" alongside the server block, but set mcp_server_name:"ticket-search-sse" to point at the SSE endpoint variant of the server.
rationale_a: This correctly adds the required mcp_toolset entry, but the objective flaw is the mismatched name: mcp_server_name must equal the name declared in mcp_servers ("ticket-search"), not an invented variant, or the toolset references no declared server.
choice_b: Leave the request as-is; the mcp_servers block alone is sufficient and the 400 just means you must retry with tool_choice:{type:"any"} so Claude is forced to discover and call the server's tools.
rationale_b: Declaring the server alone is precisely the 400 cause; tool_choice controls whether a tool must be used but cannot expose tools that were never declared via a mcp_toolset entry.
choice_c: Keep the mcp_servers block and ALSO add a matching entry to tools: tools=[{type:"mcp_toolset", mcp_server_name:"ticket-search"}], where mcp_server_name equals the name in the mcp_servers block. Both the server declaration and the toolset reference are required together.
rationale_c: The Messages API MCP connector requires BOTH mcp_servers (declaring the server) AND a tools entry of type mcp_toolset whose mcp_server_name matches the server name; declaring the server alone is a 400.
choice_d: Remove the mcp_servers block entirely and instead attach the server through a vault by passing vault_ids on the Messages request, since MCP auth and servers live in vaults.
rationale_d: Vaults hold MCP auth for managed Agents, not for the Messages API connector; the Messages connector still requires mcp_servers plus a matching mcp_toolset tools entry.

stem: You are architecting a high-volume document-triage agent on Claude Sonnet 4.6 for a logistics firm called Meridian Freight. Each request reuses an identical 22-tool toolset and a 9,000-token frozen system prompt of routing rules, then appends one short, always-different shipment-description question. The team enables prompt caching to cut cost, but after deploying they observe usage.cache_read_input_tokens sitting near zero on almost every call — the cache keeps missing. A reviewer notes that the request is being assembled by concatenating the per-request shipment question first (because it was treated as the "primary" input), followed by the system prompt, followed by the tool definitions, with a single cache_control breakpoint placed at the very end. Which change correctly explains the misses and fixes them so the stable prefix is reused?
anti_pattern: Treating the cache prefix as if components can be cached independently or in an arbitrary order, and placing volatile content (a varying user question, a per-request timestamp) ahead of stable content (tool definitions, frozen system prompt) — which silently invalidates the cached suffix on every request because prefix matching is order-sensitive and any byte change in the prefix busts everything after it.
choice_a: The order is wrong — reorder to tools → frozen system prompt → the varying shipment question — but the real fix is that each request needs a fresh cache_control breakpoint placed at the very end of the message (after the shipment question) so the newest content is always cached for next time.
rationale_a: The reorder is correct, but placing the breakpoint after the volatile shipment question is the objective flaw: the breakpoint must sit at the end of the STABLE prefix (after the system prompt, before the varying question), because content after the last breakpoint is not part of the reusable prefix and a question that changes every request can never produce a prefix match.
choice_b: Order does not matter for prompt caching because each component (tools, system, messages) is cached as an independent segment. The misses are caused by the default 5-minute TTL expiring between Meridian's bursty requests; switch the breakpoint to cache_control:{type:"ephemeral", ttl:"1h"} and the existing assembly order will start hitting.
rationale_b: Caching is prefix-based and order-sensitive, not per-segment-independent; a longer TTL cannot rescue a prefix whose leading bytes (the varying question) change every request, so this misdiagnoses the cause and the cache would still miss.
choice_c: The request is assembled in the wrong order: prompt-cache prefix matching is order-sensitive and renders tools, then system, then messages, so stable content must come first. Reorder assembly to tools → frozen system prompt → the varying shipment question, and put the cache breakpoint after the system prompt (before the volatile question). Because the volatile question was leading the prefix, any change to it busted the entire cache; with the question last, the tools+system prefix stays byte-stable and is served as cache_read.
rationale_c: Cache hits depend on a byte-stable PREFIX, and the canonical render order is tools→system→messages; placing stable content (tools, frozen system prompt) first and the varying question last keeps the prefix identical across requests so it is reused as cache_read.
choice_d: The toolset is the problem: 22 tools exceeds the prompt-cache limit of 4 cacheable components (you may only have 4 cache breakpoints), so the tool definitions overflow and cannot be cached. Reduce the agent to 4 tools or fewer and the cache will begin reading the prefix.
rationale_d: The "max 4" limit is on cache breakpoints, not on the number of tools, and tool count has no bearing on prefix stability; the real cause is the volatile question leading the prefix, so cutting tools would not fix the misses.
